q: A data engineer needs to process real-time clickstream data with sub-second latency. Which AWS service is best suited for this use case?
options: Amazon S3 event notifications
AWS Glue Spark Jobs
AWS Batch for scheduled batch workloads without sub-second latency requirements
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams
explain: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams is purpose-built for real-time streaming data with sub-second latency, making it the correct choice for clickstream processing where millisecond response times matter. AWS Glue Spark Jobs are batch-oriented and take minutes to spin up, not suitable for sub-second SLAs. S3 event notifications trigger asynchronous workflows with no latency guarantees, and AWS Batch is explicitly designed for batch workloads. Kinesis allows consumers like Lambda or Kinesis Data Analytics to process records within milliseconds of ingestion.

q: A data engineer needs to process batch data with AWS Glue. What is the minimum DPU allocation?
options: 1 DPU
10 DPU
0.5 DPU
explain: AWS Glue supports G.025X (0.0625 DPU minimum for Python shells), G.1X, and G.2X worker types. For standard Spark batch jobs, 0.5 DPU is the minimum spark job allocation. Choosing the correct DPU size prevents paying for idle compute while ensuring sufficient memory for the dataset size.

q: A data engineer implements CDC from Oracle to S3. What is the primary challenge?
options: Raw S3 storage cost, which is negligible compared to the operational overhead
Handling schema changes and late arrivals
Licensing cost for third-party CDC tools, which dominates the total cost of ownership
Network bandwidth between the Glue workers and the S3 source bucket
explain: CDC from relational databases must handle schema evolution (column additions, type changes) and out-of-order change events that arrive due to network delays. AWS DMS handles these with LOB support and transformation rules. Failing to account for schema drift causes pipeline failures when the source schema changes without notice.

q: A data engineer processes multi-format data sources. What Glue capability helps abstract differences?
options: DynamicFrames
Use only Glue Crawlers for schema detection without registering tables in the Data Catalog
AWS Glue does not support this transformation pattern natively
Catalogs only
explain: AWS Glue DynamicFrames use schema-on-read, inferring the schema at runtime from diverse file formats (JSON, CSV, Parquet, ORC, Avro) without requiring a pre-defined catalog schema. DynamicFrames also handle inconsistencies like missing fields and mixed types through the ChoiceType mechanism. Catalogs alone store discovered metadata but cannot abstract format differences during ETL execution.

q: A data engineer handles late-arriving data in streaming. What windowing approach is appropriate?
options: Process all events as an unbounded stream without any time-based grouping or aggregation
Windows with allowed lateness
Tumbling windows only
Process all events without any deduplication and accept duplicate records in results
explain: Windowing with allowed lateness is the correct approach for late-arriving streaming data. Streaming frameworks like Apache Flink (Kinesis Data Analytics) use watermarks to track event-time progress, and an allowed lateness configuration waits a defined period after the watermark before finalizing window computations. Tumbling windows without allowed lateness silently drop records that arrive after the window closes.

q: A data engineering team uses Kinesis Data Analytics for Apache Flink to process streaming clickstream events. They need to count unique users per 5-minute window, but late-arriving events (up to 2 minutes late) must be included in the correct window. Which Flink configuration achieves this?
options: Use a tumbling event-time window of 5 minutes with a watermark strategy allowing 2-minute lateness — late elements update the window result
Use a sliding window of 7 minutes sliding every 5 minutes
Use a tumbling window of 5 minutes with no watermark configuration
Use a session window with a 2-minute gap timeout
explain: Event-time tumbling windows with a 2-minute allowed lateness watermark allow Flink to include late-arriving records in the correct 5-minute window. The watermark tracks event time progress and allows elements up to 2 minutes late to update previously emitted window results. Tumbling windows without watermarks drop late data. Session windows group events by inactivity gaps, not fixed time intervals. Sliding windows overlap and would double-count users.

q: A data engineer implements deduplication in streaming. What state mechanism is needed?
options: Distributed cache tracking record IDs
Use a single EC2 instance as the Kinesis consumer to simplify operational management
Stateless processing without any record ID tracking across distributed workers
Maintain the deduplication state only in Lambda in-memory dictionaries within each function instance
explain: Distributed state stores (DynamoDB with TTL, Apache Flink's managed state, or Redis) enable deduplication across multiple parallel streaming consumers by providing a shared, consistent view of seen record IDs. In-memory deduplication within a single consumer fails when the stream is partitioned across multiple workers that each see only a subset of records.

q: A data engineer processes IoT data with high cardinality. What Kinesis feature helps?
options: Enhanced fan-out
Resharding Kinesis shards every hour based on traffic patterns provides sufficient scaling
Retention period
Batch size is the only optimization lever for Lambda processing of DynamoDB Streams events
explain: Kinesis Enhanced Fan-Out provides each registered consumer its own dedicated 2 MB/s read throughput per shard, allowing multiple downstream consumers (IoT analytics, alerting, archival) to process the same telemetry stream independently without competing for the shared standard 2 MB/s per-shard limit. Standard GetRecords consumers share the 2 MB/s read bandwidth, causing starvation in high-cardinality IoT multi-consumer scenarios.

q: A data engineer needs to validate data during transformation. What Glue feature helps?
options: Perform data quality checks manually by reviewing S3 file samples after each pipeline run
Use only Glue Crawlers for schema detection without registering tables in the Data Catalog
Data Quality rules
Skip all data quality checks to maximize pipeline throughput and minimize latency
explain: AWS Glue Data Quality provides a rule-based framework for defining data quality constraints (completeness, uniqueness, referential integrity, value ranges) that execute as part of the Glue ETL job. Rules can fail the job, write results to S3, or emit CloudWatch metrics, enabling automated data validation without writing custom validation code.

q: A data engineering team needs to enforce row-level security on an S3 data lake so that sales representatives can only query rows for their assigned region. The data is cataloged in AWS Glue and queried via Athena. Which solution provides this without modifying query code?
options: Use S3 bucket policies with condition keys for each region
Use Athena workgroup settings to filter rows by region
Create separate S3 prefixes per region and use IAM policies to restrict access per prefix
Use AWS Lake Formation row-level security filters — define data filter expressions per IAM principal or role that automatically apply when querying via Athena or Redshift Spectrum
explain: Lake Formation row-level security allows defining data filter expressions (e.g., region = 'WEST') that are automatically enforced when principals query data via Athena or Redshift Spectrum. Filters are transparently applied without query modification. S3 bucket policies and separate prefixes require copying or reorganizing data. Athena workgroups control query execution settings and cost, not row-level data access.

q: A data engineer orchestrates a multi-step ETL pipeline. Which service provides serverless orchestration?
options: EC2 instances
CloudWatch Events only
AWS Step Functions with error handling and retries
Manual scheduling
explain: AWS Step Functions orchestrates complex multi-step ETL pipelines with built-in retry logic, error catching, parallel execution, and conditional branching. Unlike Lambda chaining (which loses state between steps), Step Functions persists execution state and provides full audit history of every step transition, making debugging failed pipelines significantly easier.

q: What is the primary advantage of using Apache Airflow (MWAA) for data pipelines?
options: Directed acyclic graph (DAG) scheduling with dependency management
Columnar formats provide no query performance improvement over row-based storage in S3
Real-time streaming
Cost savings is the primary reason to implement row-level security in Lake Formation
explain: Apache Airflow DAGs (Directed Acyclic Graphs) express complex task dependencies, support conditional execution paths, and can be generated dynamically using Python code. MWAA (Managed Workflows for Apache Airflow) adds the operational benefits of a managed service — scaling, patching, and high availability — while preserving Airflow's full feature set for complex scheduling requirements.

q: A data engineer implements event-driven architecture. What is the recommended pattern?
options: Manual invocation
Polling continuously
Scheduled jobs only
EventBridge to trigger Lambda/Step Functions on S3 or source events
explain: Amazon EventBridge provides a serverless event bus that routes events to targets (Lambda, Step Functions, SQS, SNS) based on rule patterns matching event content. For event-driven data architectures, EventBridge decouples producers from consumers, allowing multiple downstream pipelines to react to source events independently without tight coupling between services.

q: A company runs ETL jobs on Amazon EMR. Jobs are triggered by S3 file arrivals and sometimes fail due to transient errors. The team wants jobs to automatically retry with exponential backoff and send alerts on persistent failures, without managing a custom scheduler. Which orchestration solution is most appropriate?
options: Use a cron job on an EC2 instance to monitor S3 and trigger EMR
Use Apache Airflow on MWAA with S3 sensors and EMR operators
Use Amazon EventBridge to trigger Lambda which starts EMR clusters
Use AWS Step Functions with a state machine that triggers EMR steps, implements Retry policies with exponential backoff per state, and uses Catch to route persistent failures to SNS
explain: Step Functions provides built-in Retry configuration with exponential backoff and MaxAttempts per state. Catch handlers route to error notification (SNS) after all retries fail. It integrates natively with EMR via StartJobRun API. MWAA/Airflow also works but requires cluster management. EventBridge + Lambda lacks built-in retry orchestration for multi-step workflows. EC2 cron jobs are operationally fragile.

q: A data engineer needs to trigger pipelines on data arrival. What is the best approach?
options: Scheduled jobs always
S3 event notifications to SQS/SNS or EventBridge
Trigger all Glue jobs manually from the AWS console based on a fixed daily schedule
Polling every minute
explain: S3 event notifications (delivered to SQS, SNS, or Lambda, or via EventBridge) trigger pipeline execution within seconds of data landing in S3. This eliminates polling delays and wasted compute cycles inherent in scheduled pipeline approaches, enabling near-real-time processing for data that arrives at unpredictable intervals.

q: A data engineer implements data quality checks in a pipeline. Where should checks be placed?
options: Insert validation checkpoints at random pipeline stages based on traffic load
At each transformation stage with alerting
At the end only
Avoid all validation checks in production pipelines to maximize throughput
explain: Implementing data quality checks at each transformation stage (not just at the end) catches errors closest to where they are introduced, reducing the cost of correction. A record with a null required field caught at ingestion requires simple rejection; the same error discovered after 6 downstream transformations requires expensive root-cause investigation and reprocessing.

q: A data analyst needs to query data stored in both Amazon S3 (Parquet files) and Amazon DynamoDB from a single Athena query to join customer profiles (DynamoDB) with transaction history (S3). What enables this?
options: Use Athena Federated Query with the DynamoDB connector — enables Athena to query DynamoDB tables directly and join results with S3 data in a single SQL query
Copy DynamoDB data to S3 daily and join in Athena
Export DynamoDB to S3 using DynamoDB export feature and join with Athena
Use AWS Glue to synchronize DynamoDB to an RDS database, then query RDS from Athena
explain: Athena Federated Query uses Lambda-based data source connectors to query non-S3 data sources (DynamoDB, RDS, CloudWatch Logs, etc.) directly. The DynamoDB connector is provided by AWS. Federated query results can be joined with S3 tables in a single SQL statement. Daily copies add latency and storage cost. DynamoDB export to S3 is batch/offline, not real-time. Glue sync to RDS adds complexity.

q: A data engineer creates CloudWatch alarms for pipelines. What threshold is appropriate?
options: SLA-based thresholds with owner notifications
High thresholds only
Avoid setting any CloudWatch alarms and rely on manual log inspection for issues
Random thresholds
explain: SLA-based CloudWatch alarm thresholds — defined based on the maximum acceptable data latency or error rate agreed in the service-level agreement — ensure that alerts fire when the pipeline is at risk of breaching commitments. Static thresholds (e.g., alert at 100 errors) are less effective because they do not account for normal traffic variance that would make 100 errors acceptable at high volume but critical at low volume.

q: A data engineer implements incremental data loads. What tracking mechanism is essential?
options: Manual tracking
Maintain last-processed timestamp or sequence number
Full reload daily
Execute full table scans on every pipeline run without maintaining any state
explain: Maintaining a last-processed timestamp or sequence number (stored in DynamoDB, S3, or the source system's checkpoint table) enables incremental data loads that read only new or modified records since the previous run. Without this marker, pipelines must perform full scans of the source system on every execution — O(n) cost per run instead of O(delta).

q: A financial company stores sensitive customer financial data in a Redshift cluster. Their compliance team requires that specific columns containing SSNs and account numbers be masked for junior analysts while remaining visible to senior analysts. Which solution achieves this within Redshift?
options: Use Redshift dynamic data masking — define masking policies on sensitive columns and attach them to roles, so junior analysts see masked values while senior analysts see original data in the same query
Use AWS Macie to automatically mask sensitive data in Redshift
Create separate Redshift clusters for junior and senior analysts
Create views with CASE statements that mask data for junior analysts
explain: Amazon Redshift dynamic data masking (DDM) applies column-level masking policies based on role. Junior analysts get a masked version (e.g., XXX-XX-1234) while senior analysts see the original data — transparently, using the same table and queries. Views with CASE statements work but require maintaining separate view objects for each role combination. Separate clusters are expensive. Macie discovers sensitive data in S3, not Redshift.

q: A data engineer implements pipeline notifications. What is the recommended approach?
options: Manual checks only
SNS/SQS integration with Step Functions for alerts
Poll an email inbox to receive notification of pipeline failures rather than SNS
No notifications
explain: Integrating SNS or SQS into Step Functions error-handling states enables automated pipeline failure notifications to on-call engineers, PagerDuty, or ticketing systems without building custom alerting logic. Step Functions' Catch states can send the error details and execution ARN directly to an SNS topic, which fans out to multiple subscribers.

q: A data engineering team ingests JSON events from 10,000 IoT sensors into Kinesis Data Firehose, delivering to S3. Query performance with Athena is poor because each sensor writes a tiny JSON file every second, creating millions of small files per day. What Firehose configuration improves query performance?
options: Configure Firehose buffer size to 128 MB and buffer interval to 300 seconds, enable dynamic partitioning by sensor_type, and enable Snappy compression — larger batched files with columnar compression dramatically improve Athena scan performance
Use Kinesis Data Analytics to aggregate events before writing to S3
Increase the number of Kinesis shards
Enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering on the destination bucket
explain: Kinesis Firehose's buffer size (up to 128 MB) and interval (up to 900 seconds) controls how data is batched before writing to S3. Larger buffers create fewer, larger files — ideal for Athena which has per-file overhead. Dynamic partitioning routes records to S3 prefixes based on data fields (e.g., sensor_type). Compression reduces scan costs. Shard count affects Kinesis Data Streams write throughput. S3 Intelligent-Tiering manages storage class, not file size. KDA aggregation adds complexity and latency.

q: What is the best practice for handling slowly changing dimensions in pipelines?
options: Implement SCD Type 2 with version and effective dates
Overwrite daily
Ignore schema changes at the source and let them cause pipeline failures for detection
Perform a complete data refresh on every pipeline run to avoid incremental tracking complexity
explain: SCD Type 2 (Slowly Changing Dimension Type 2) adds a new row for each change, preserving the complete history of dimension changes. Each row includes valid_from and valid_to effective dates and a current_flag, allowing queries to retrieve the dimension value as of any historical date. This is essential for analytical queries that need point-in-time accuracy (e.g., 'what was the customer's plan tier when they made this purchase?').

q: A data engineer implements encryption for data lakes. What is the recommended approach?
options: Store all data lake objects in plaintext and rely on IAM access control exclusively
S3 KMS encryption at rest + TLS for transit
Encryption at rest only
Application-level encryption
explain: Dual-layer encryption — S3 KMS SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS for data at rest combined with TLS (HTTPS) for data in transit — provides comprehensive protection against two distinct threat vectors. Encryption at rest protects against unauthorized access to S3 objects (if bucket permissions are misconfigured or physical media is compromised). TLS in transit protects against man-in-the-middle interception during data transfer.

q: A data engineer implements column-level security in Lake Formation. What is a use case?
options: Protect entire datasets
Hide PII columns from non-authorized users while showing other data
Performance optimization
Cost reduction is the sole motivation for using Glue DynamicFrames over DataFrames
explain: AWS Lake Formation column-level security allows data lake administrators to grant column-specific access within a table, hiding sensitive columns (SSN, salary, medical record IDs) from users who have access to other columns in the same table. Without column-level security, granting access to a table exposes all columns, forcing teams to create separate views or tables to enforce column-level access control.

q: How should a data engineer handle data governance in multi-team environments?
options: Allow all teams unrestricted access to all data lake assets without any policies
Skip governance controls to reduce pipeline complexity and improve development velocity
Manual spreadsheets
Lake Formation resource-based permissions with data lake administration
explain: AWS Lake Formation resource-based permissions provide centralized, team-level access control for data lake assets (databases, tables, columns, rows). Administrators grant data lake permissions to IAM roles, which each team uses — simplifying access management compared to S3 bucket policies that require coordinating access at the object level across all team members.

q: A data engineer implements row-level security in Lake Formation. What is a scenario?
options: Encrypting all data solves the row-level security requirement without database-level policies
This pattern has no valid application in AWS data engineering architectures
Sales reps see only their region's data rows
Use database views in Redshift to hide columns rather than Lake Formation policies
explain: Row-level security in Lake Formation uses data filter expressions that restrict which rows a given IAM role can query. For example, a regional sales representative's IAM role has a data filter restricting their Athena queries to rows where region = 'West', preventing them from seeing data for other regions even in tables they have full column access to.

q: A data engineer implements data masking for PII. What approach is recommended?
options: Delete all PII-containing records before processing rather than masking or transforming them
Move to separate storage
Store all sensitive fields in plaintext and restrict access via IAM policies alone
Use Glue Data Quality or custom transforms to mask sensitive values
explain: Data masking in AWS Glue uses custom transformations or Data Quality rules to replace sensitive field values with masked representations (hash, asterisks, format-preserving encryption) before writing to the target. Masking at ingestion ensures that sensitive values are never stored in plaintext in the data lake, where downstream access controls may be less rigorous than at the source.

q: How should a data engineer implement least privilege in data pipelines?
options: IAM roles with minimum required permissions; Lake Formation resource-level policies
No access controls
Enable public S3 bucket access for the data lake to simplify cross-team data sharing
Grant admin access to all
explain: Least privilege in data pipelines means granting each IAM role only the specific S3 prefix, Glue database, Redshift schema, or DynamoDB table it needs — not broad wildcard permissions. Combined with Lake Formation resource-level policies, least privilege limits the blast radius of a compromised role to only the data it was authorized to access, preventing lateral movement across the data lake.

q: A data engineer processes medical records. What Glue capability enables sensitivity handling?
options: Random data sampling to identify approximate PII patterns without full-dataset scans
Encryption only
Data Quality for PII detection
Column encryption at rest using KMS without any detection or classification step
explain: AWS Glue Data Quality rules can detect PII patterns using regular expressions (SSN format, email patterns, phone number formats) and apply transformations (masking, hashing, removal) when PII is detected. This provides an automated, auditable PII handling mechanism within the ETL pipeline, reducing the risk of PII propagating to downstream analytical stores where access controls are less strict.

q: A data engineer needs real-time order processing. What architecture pattern is optimal?
options: Batch all real-time events and process them in a nightly Glue ETL job
Use scheduled Glue jobs running every hour to approximate near-real-time processing
Manual processing
Event-driven Kinesis to Lambda to DynamoDB
explain: An event-driven architecture using Kinesis Data Streams → Lambda → DynamoDB enables sub-second order processing by eliminating polling delays. Each order event on the Kinesis stream triggers an immediate Lambda invocation that processes and writes the order to DynamoDB atomically. Batch Glue jobs add hours of latency. Scheduled Glue jobs add up to an hour. Manual processing does not scale.

q: A data engineer implements SLA monitoring. What metric defines data freshness SLA?
options: Storage size consumed is the primary metric for evaluating Glue DPU utilization
Maximum acceptable time lag between source update and analytics readiness
Error rate only
Track the number of Athena queries executed as the primary cost control metric
explain: Data freshness SLAs define the maximum acceptable delay between data creation at the source and its availability in the target analytical system — measured as event_time → landing_time lag. Tracking this metric enables proactive CloudWatch alerting before the SLA is breached. Error rate alone does not capture freshness degradation — a pipeline can have zero errors while running hours behind schedule.

q: A data engineer monitors Glue job costs. What optimization is most impactful?
options: Rightsize DPU allocation based on execution profiles
Minimize DPU
Use maximum DPU always
Provision maximum DPU for all Glue jobs and optimize cost as a post-launch activity
explain: Rightsizing DPU allocation based on actual job execution profiles is the most impactful Glue cost optimization because each DPU (4 vCPU, 16 GB RAM) is billed per second. Profiling CPU and memory utilization via Glue CloudWatch metrics identifies whether a job is compute-bound or I/O-bound and guides reduction. Simply minimizing DPU blindly can cause job failures or excessive runtime; profiling-guided rightsizing achieves the cost reduction safely.

q: What is the purpose of implementing circuit breakers in data pipelines?
options: Prevent cascading failures by stopping requests to unhealthy systems
Increase throughput
Serves no practical use case beyond what existing services already provide
Performance
explain: Circuit breakers in data pipelines prevent cascading failures by temporarily stopping requests to a downstream component when its error rate exceeds a threshold. The circuit 'opens' and the pipeline routes to a dead-letter queue or fallback sink, preventing an already-struggling system from being overwhelmed by retried requests. Once the downstream system recovers, the circuit closes and normal delivery resumes.

q: A data engineer implements column masking in Athena. What configuration is required?
options: Store all sensitive fields in plaintext and restrict access via IAM policies alone
Application logic
Run manual Athena queries against column-level metadata to verify masking configuration
Lake Formation column-level security with Athena integration
explain: AWS Lake Formation column-level security controls which Athena columns are visible to each IAM role. When an analyst queries a Glue-cataloged table through Athena, Lake Formation intercepts the query and filters out restricted columns transparently — no view creation needed. This is enforced regardless of how the table is queried (Athena console, JDBC, API). IAM policies alone cannot restrict individual columns within a query.

q: A data engineer implements Lake Formation table permissions. What is the benefit?
options: Cost reduction is the primary benefit of cross-account Lake Formation data sharing
Provides no measurable advantage over manual configuration management approaches
Performance
Centralized table-level access control superseding S3 bucket policies
explain: AWS Lake Formation table permissions supersede S3 bucket policies for services that integrate with Lake Formation (Athena, Redshift Spectrum, EMR with Lake Formation integration). Access control is defined once at the logical table level and applies consistently across all query engines — eliminating the need to duplicate and synchronize S3 bucket policies for every new service integration. Performance is not a Lake Formation benefit; governance and centralized control are.

q: A data engineer handles nested JSON in Glue. What transformation approach is best?
options: Convert nested JSON to CSV first using a Lambda pre-processor before Glue ingestion
Glue DynamicFrame with flatten/explode operations
Manual parsing
String operations
explain: AWS Glue DynamicFrames handle nested JSON structures using the Relationalize transform, which automatically flattens nested objects and explodes arrays into separate DataFrames without requiring custom parsing code. Manual JSON parsing requires writing recursive logic for each unique schema — error-prone and unmaintainable when the source schema evolves. Pre-converting to CSV loses the nested structure.

q: What is the recommended approach for data validation in streaming pipelines?
options: Post-processing only
Schema validation at ingestion with metrics tracking failures
Skip all data quality checks to maximize pipeline throughput and minimize latency
Perform data quality checks manually by reviewing S3 file samples after each pipeline run
explain: Validating data at ingestion — before it enters the streaming pipeline — catches malformed records, type mismatches, and schema violations closest to the source, where they are cheapest to remediate. Post-processing validation allows bad data to propagate through the pipeline and potentially contaminate downstream aggregations or trigger silent errors in consumer applications.

q: A data engineer optimizes Lambda for data processing. What is critical?
options: Minimize cold starts with container reuse and reserve concurrency
Increase timeout to max
Unlimited resources
No optimization
explain: Lambda cold starts add 300ms–2s of latency on the first invocation of each new container instance. Minimizing cold starts through provisioned concurrency (pre-warming containers), initializing SDK clients and connections in global scope (reused across warm invocations), and minimizing deployment package size is critical for latency-sensitive data pipeline processing. Increasing timeout has no effect on cold start duration.

q: A data engineer implements columnar storage in S3. What format offers best performance?
options: XML
JSON
CSV
Parquet with Snappy compression
explain: Apache Parquet with Snappy compression combines columnar storage (read only the columns needed for a query) with efficient compression to deliver both reduced storage cost and faster Athena query performance. JSON and CSV are row-based formats requiring full row scans even for single-column queries. Parquet also enables partition pruning and predicate pushdown, further reducing data scanned per query.

q: A data engineer implements row-level access in RDS. What mechanism is used?
options: No row-level security
There are no valid AWS-native solutions for this data processing scenario
VPD (Virtual Private Database) or stored procedures with row filtering
S3 only
explain: Row-level security in RDS is implemented using native database engine features: Row-Level Security (RLS) policies in RDS for PostgreSQL, Virtual Private Database (VPD) in RDS for Oracle, or row-filtering stored procedures in RDS for MySQL. These mechanisms enforce row access based on the database session context (current user, role, application variable), ensuring consistent access control regardless of how the application queries the database.

q: A data engineer processes IoT telemetry with duplicates. What Kinesis feature enables deduplication?
options: No deduplication
Group all streaming events into daily batches before writing to the target data store
Deduplication ID in record key
Enhanced Fan-Out
explain: Kinesis uses partition keys to route records from the same sensor to the same shard, enabling deduplication within that shard's consumer using a record ID map. A partition key combining sensor_id and time_bucket ensures all retransmissions of the same reading go to the same shard and consumer instance, where the deduplication map is maintained in memory with a TTL matching the retransmission window.

q: A data engineer processes variable-rate streams. What Kinesis pattern works?
options: Manual resharding
No adaptation
Adaptive scaling with enhanced fan-out
Keep the Kinesis shard count fixed and accept throughput exceptions during traffic peaks
explain: Kinesis Data Streams On-Demand mode provides adaptive capacity scaling — shard capacity scales up automatically in response to traffic increases without manual shard management or UpdateShardCount API calls. Combined with enhanced fan-out for consumers, this handles variable-rate workloads like seasonal spikes without engineering intervention. Fixed shard counts will throttle during peak traffic that exceeds provisioned capacity.

q: A data engineer masks sensitive fields during ingestion. What approach?
options: No masking
Glue Data Quality patterns at ingestion
Post-processing
Manually inspect each record for schema compliance before writing to the target
explain: Applying data masking at ingestion time using AWS Glue transformation rules or Lambda preprocessing ensures that sensitive field values (credit card numbers, SSNs, medical record IDs) are never stored in plaintext in the data lake. Masking at ingestion closes the exposure window — post-ingestion masking leaves unmasked PII accessible in raw storage during the gap, which may violate regulatory requirements.

q: A data engineer handles Glue crawler classification errors. What to do?
options: Use default AWS service configurations for all pipeline components without optimization
Custom classifiers with pattern definitions
Manually review each data record for PII before allowing it to enter the pipeline
No classification
explain: When Glue Crawlers cannot classify files using built-in classifiers (XML, JSON, CSV, Parquet), custom classifiers — defined using regex patterns for row format, SerDe libraries, or Grok patterns for semi-structured logs — extend Glue's classification capability to proprietary or uncommon formats. Without custom classifiers, unrecognized files are stored as UNKNOWN in the Data Catalog without schema inference, preventing downstream ETL jobs from reading the data correctly.

q: A data engineer processes encrypted Kinesis messages. What is required?
options: The operation succeeds because cross-account access is automatically granted by AWS
KMS key permissions in Lambda/Glue consumers
Manual key
Store encrypted data in the pipeline without ever decrypting it for processing
explain: Consuming server-side encrypted Kinesis records requires the consumer's IAM role to have kms:Decrypt permission for the KMS Customer Managed Key (CMK) used to encrypt the stream. The KMS key policy must also explicitly allow the consumer role as a principal — IAM permissions alone are insufficient if the key policy denies access. Without this configuration, the consumer receives ciphertext and cannot process the records.

q: A data engineer handles late-arriving events in time windows. What approach?
options: Discard late-arriving events immediately and log a warning metric for monitoring
No handling
Configure allowed lateness in Flink/Spark
Manually inspect each record for schema compliance before writing to the target
explain: Configuring allowed lateness in Apache Flink (Kinesis Data Analytics) instructs the streaming engine to buffer events arriving after the watermark deadline for a defined tolerance period before finalizing window results. Events arriving within the lateness window update the result; events arriving beyond the tolerance are discarded or routed to a side output. Discarding late events immediately causes data loss for events that arrive slightly out of order due to network variance.

q: A data engineer handles variable Kinesis message sizes. What consideration?
options: Batch size calculation; payload limits
Random sizes
No consideration
Use fixed batch sizes regardless of record size and accept occasional payload limit errors
explain: Kinesis Data Streams enforces a 1 MB maximum record size (including partition key). Producers must calculate batch sizes to avoid exceeding this limit; large messages must be split into chunks with sequence numbers or compressed with gzip/Snappy. The PutRecords API batches up to 500 records (max 5 MB total) per call, so very small records benefit from batching to reduce API call cost, while very large records must be proactively split.

q: A data engineer needs multi-region DynamoDB. What feature enables this?
options: Global Tables with active-active replication
Read replicas
Cross-region copies
No feature
explain: Amazon DynamoDB Global Tables provide active-active multi-region replication, allowing reads and writes in all configured regions simultaneously. Global Tables use last-writer-wins conflict resolution for concurrent writes to the same item from different regions. DynamoDB does not support read replicas (an RDS concept) — Global Tables is the native solution for multi-region DynamoDB deployments.

q: A data engineer stores sensitive Redshift data. What encryption is needed?
options: Store all intermediate state exclusively in S3 without using any in-memory processing
HSM for key management; column-level security
Store all data lake objects in plaintext and rely on IAM access control exclusively
Application level
explain: For highly sensitive Redshift data, AWS supports HSM (Hardware Security Module) integration for managing the cluster encryption master key outside of AWS KMS — providing separation of key management from data storage required by regulated industries. Combined with Redshift column-level security (Dynamic Data Masking or column-level access control), this restricts access to sensitive columns even for authenticated database users.

q: A data engineer stores time-series data in S3. What partitioning is optimal?
options: Single partition
No partitioning
Assign partition keys randomly to distribute records evenly regardless of the data access pattern
Year/Month/Day with time bucketing
explain: Partitioning S3 time-series data using a hierarchical key structure (year=YYYY/month=MM/day=DD or /hour=HH) enables Athena partition pruning — queries filtering on specific time ranges skip all partitions outside the filter, dramatically reducing data scanned and query cost. Random partitioning distributes data evenly but prevents time-based pruning. No partitioning requires scanning all data for every time-range query.

q: A data engineer monitors Glue worker performance. What metric matters?
options: Network bandwidth between Glue workers and the S3 data source
Executor memory and GC time
Job count
Disk I/O throughput on the Glue worker EBS volumes as the sole performance metric
explain: AWS Glue Spark worker performance is most effectively monitored using executor memory utilization and GC (Garbage Collection) time metrics. High GC time indicates memory pressure — the JVM is spending excessive time reclaiming heap instead of processing data, causing job slowdowns. The remediation is to upgrade to G.2X workers (16 GB RAM vs G.1X's 8 GB) or reduce per-task data volume through better partitioning.

q: A data engineer implements column-level encryption in Redshift. What is needed?
options: Store all data lake objects in plaintext and rely on IAM access control exclusively
Application only
HSM with column mask expressions
Manual
explain: Column-level encryption in Amazon Redshift uses HSM (Hardware Security Module) integration or KMS-backed custom encryption functions to encrypt specific columns (SSN, credit card numbers, salary) at the data level. Even users with table SELECT permission cannot read sensitive column values without the decryption key. Redshift Dynamic Data Masking (DDM) can complement this by masking values at query time for users who lack decryption authorization.

q: A data engineer implements lifecycle policies for compliance. What approach?
options: Retain all historical CloudWatch metrics indefinitely without any retention policy
Skip S3 lifecycle policies and manually archive objects using Lambda on a schedule
Transition to Glacier; delete after retention period
Manual
explain: S3 Lifecycle policies automate compliance retention by transitioning objects to Glacier (lower-cost archival) after a defined period and deleting objects after the required retention period expires. This provides an automated, auditable trail that satisfies compliance requirements. Manual deletion processes are error-prone and cannot be independently verified by auditors. Combined with S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode, immutable retention is enforced even against root account deletion.

q: A data engineer implements data masking at query time. What Lake Formation feature helps?
options: Manual
Dynamic masking with column-level security filters
Application only
Store all sensitive fields in plaintext and restrict access via IAM policies alone
explain: AWS Lake Formation dynamic data masking masks sensitive column values at query time based on the requesting IAM role. Analysts see masked values (e.g., XXX-XX-1234 for SSNs) while authorized roles see actual values — all from the same Athena query against the same table. This eliminates the operational complexity of maintaining separate masked views per access tier, as masking is enforced transparently at the Lake Formation layer.

q: A data engineer audits Athena workgroup usage. What metric tracks this?
options: Track the number of Athena queries executed as the primary cost control metric
Manual
Execute full table scans on every pipeline run without maintaining any state
CloudTrail workgroup API calls; cost per workgroup
explain: AWS CloudTrail logs every Athena API call (StartQueryExecution, GetQueryResults) with the IAM principal, query string, workgroup name, and bytes scanned. AWS Cost Explorer and CloudWatch metrics provide Athena spending broken down by workgroup based on bytes scanned and the per-TB pricing. These two sources together provide a complete audit picture of who is querying what data, when, and at what cost per workgroup.

q: A data engineer ingests 1M events/sec via Kinesis with 100 shards. What autoscaling strategy handles 10x growth?
options: Disable autoscaling
Use resharding on-demand with metrics-based triggers; implement enhanced fan-out for consumers
Pre-scale to 1000 shards immediately
Switch to Firehose only
explain: Metrics-based resharding (CloudWatch alarm on IncomingBytes → UpdateShardCount API) scales shard count dynamically as traffic grows to 10M events/sec. Kinesis enhanced fan-out provides each registered consumer a dedicated 2 MB/s read throughput per shard, decoupling consumer read performance from producer write scaling. Pre-scaling to 1,000 shards immediately wastes cost during low-traffic periods.

q: A Glue ETL job transforms 100 GB CSV to Parquet with 16 DPU, taking 20 minutes at 15% utilization. What is the bottleneck?
options: Insufficient DPU allocation
I/O bound (S3 read/write); optimize partitioning, compression, or add prefixes
CPU saturation
Increase DPU immediately
explain: Low DPU CPU utilization (<20%) combined with slow job completion time is a classic I/O bottleneck signature in Glue. The workers are idle waiting for S3 reads/writes rather than processing data. Optimizing S3 throughput involves: enabling S3 prefix parallelism (multiple prefixes for concurrent reads), using Parquet with predicate pushdown to reduce bytes read, and tuning shuffle partition counts to match available DPU.

q: A DMS full load from Oracle to S3 stalls after 50% with LOB handling exception. CLOBs average 500 MB. How should DMS be reconfigured?
options: Exclude LOBs entirely
Increase replication instance
Use Glue instead
Enable LOB chunking; limit LOB size or pre-process at source
explain: DMS LOB (Large Object Binary/CLOB/BLOB) handling requires special chunking configuration because LOBs exceed the standard DMS replication record buffer. Enabling LOB chunking with an appropriate chunk size (typically 32-64 KB) allows DMS to stream large objects in segments. Pre-processing at the source to remove or compress oversized LOBs prevents repeated migration failures when source data contains unexpectedly large values.

q: A Glue Data Quality rule fails for 2% of records: "amount >= 0". These failures are legitimate business data (reversals). How should the pipeline handle this?
options: Silently discard records that fail schema validation without logging or alerting
Quarantine failures to DLQ table; log with context; alert data owner for investigation
Stop pipeline; manual review
Force load anyway
explain: When a data quality rule rejects legitimate business records (reversals with negative amounts), the correct pattern is quarantine with context preservation — route failing records to a dead-letter queue (DLQ) table with the failing rule, record value, and pipeline context logged. Alerting the data owner enables rule refinement (e.g., updating the rule to allow negative amounts for reversal records). Stopping the pipeline on every legitimate reversal would halt business-critical processing.

q: A Kinesis Data Analytics application aggregates events with tumbling 1-hour windows. Events arrive with 30-second skew. Late arrivals beyond 1 hour are lost. What configuration preserves late data?
options: This problem cannot be solved with AWS managed services and requires custom hardware
Use ALLOWED LATENESS parameter (e.g., 2 hours) with sliding windows
Increase window to 2 hours
Discard all late-arriving events rather than configuring allowed lateness in the stream
explain: The ALLOWED LATENESS parameter in Apache Flink (Kinesis Data Analytics) instructs the engine to wait beyond the window's watermark before finalizing results, preserving late records in the correct window. For 30-second event skew, a 2-hour ALLOWED LATENESS ensures all delayed records are captured without dropping data. Simply increasing the window to 2 hours changes the aggregation granularity and doesn't solve the late-arrival problem.

q: A team processes data from 20 REST APIs with different rate limits and schemas. Weekly API changes require job updates. What architecture reduces maintenance?
options: Single hardcoded Glue job
Lambda + EventBridge + Secrets Manager per API; SQS for batching and rate limiting
Scheduled cron jobs on EC2 instances polling each API sequentially every hour
Manual Python scripts
explain: A modular event-driven architecture (Lambda + EventBridge per API, Secrets Manager for credentials, SQS for rate-limit buffering) reduces maintenance by isolating each API integration — updates to one API's Lambda do not affect others. SQS FIFO enforces rate limits per API by controlling message visibility. A single hardcoded Glue job requires code changes for every API schema change, creating a maintenance bottleneck.

q: A Glue job deduplicates streaming events via unique ID in 24-hour windows. Processing rate: 1M events/sec. What state management scales?
options: In-memory HashMap
Accept duplicate records in the output, trusting downstream consumers to deduplicate
Use a local file on the Lambda execution environment as the deduplication state store
DynamoDB with TTL matching dedup window; distributed state across workers
explain: At 1M events/sec with 24-hour deduplication windows, the deduplication state grows to billions of entries. DynamoDB with a TTL attribute set to the window boundary automatically expires entries after 24 hours, preventing unbounded storage growth. Distributed state across Glue workers means each worker queries DynamoDB for the canonical seen-IDs map — ensuring consistent deduplication regardless of which worker sees a duplicate. In-memory HashMaps are lost on worker restart and cannot be shared across distributed workers.

q: A Kinesis-to-RDS pipeline uses Lambda consumer. Lambda checkpoints to DynamoDB every 1000 records. Lambda crashes after 500 records without checkpointing. On retry, what happens?
options: Reprocesses from last checkpoint; application must be idempotent to avoid duplicates
Manual recovery
Skips to next 1000
The pipeline terminates permanently and requires a full restart from the source checkpoint
explain: Kinesis checkpoints record the last successfully processed sequence number in DynamoDB. When Lambda crashes after processing 500 records without updating the checkpoint, the retry starts from the last checkpoint (position 0 of the batch). This causes records 1-500 to be reprocessed — at-least-once delivery. The application must be idempotent (e.g., using upsert/conditional writes in RDS) so that reprocessing the same record produces the same result without duplication.

q: A DataBrew recipe transforms customer records: standardize phone, fill ZIPs via geo-enrichment, deduplicate. Pipeline runs daily on 100M records. What is optimal configuration?
options: Single recipe with chained transformations (standardize -> enrich -> deduplicate)
Manually trigger Glue ETL jobs using the AWS console without any automation
Three separate jobs
Perform all data quality remediation steps manually using AWS console operations
explain: AWS Glue DataBrew recipes chain multiple transformation steps (standardize, enrich, deduplicate) into a single ordered pipeline applied to each record. A single recipe is more maintainable than three separate jobs because transformation order is preserved, inter-step dependencies are explicit, and changes are versioned together. DataBrew's managed execution scales the recipe to 100M records without custom Spark code.

q: A Glue job reads JSON with nested order line items and dynamic attributes. Output must be flat Parquet for analytics. What DynamicFrame transformation is suitable?
options: flatten() with array explosion; explode() for nested rows
No transformation
Manual JSON parsing
String parsing
explain: AWS Glue DynamicFrame's flatten() method promotes nested struct fields to top-level columns, and explode() (or the Relationalize transform) expands array fields so each array element becomes its own row. This produces a flat relational schema suitable for Parquet storage and SQL analytics without writing custom recursive parsing code. Manual JSON parsing in Python is error-prone and does not leverage Glue's distributed Spark execution.

q: A MSK cluster ingests 500 MB/sec from IoT sensors. Message volume varies 10x between peak and off-peak. What partitioning strategy ensures ordering with spikes?
options: Random partitions
Hash on device_id; each partition maintains order; auto-scaling topic partitions
Single partition (no parallelism)
No partitioning
explain: Hashing device_id to the Kafka partition key ensures all messages from the same sensor land in the same partition, preserving per-device ordering for time-series analysis. MSK topic auto-scaling (via Managed Scaling or manual partition expansion) accommodates 10x volume spikes while the hash function continues routing each device to a consistent partition. Random partitioning distributes load evenly but destroys per-device ordering guarantees.

q: A Kinesis Firehose batches data: size 128 MB, time 300 sec. Ingestion rate: 100 MB/min. What is expected flush behavior and cost impact?
options: Flush every 300 sec with ~300 MB; lower per-record cost than small batches
Flush at 128 MB instantly
Process each record individually in a separate Lambda invocation without any batching
Accumulate indefinitely
explain: Kinesis Firehose flushes whichever buffer threshold is reached first: 128 MB OR 300 seconds. At 100 MB/min ingestion, the 128 MB size threshold is hit in ~76 seconds — well before the 300-second interval. So Firehose flushes approximately every 76 seconds with ~127 MB files, not every 300 seconds. Larger S3 objects reduce per-record API overhead and improve Athena query performance. Per-record Lambda invocations would eliminate buffering benefits entirely.

q: A team converts historical CSV files from Parquet to ORC for archival. ORC compresses better but ecosystem is smaller. What is the deciding factor?
options: Use both formats simultaneously and let query engines select the optimal format per query
Always ORC for compression
Storage cost only
Use Parquet for compatibility; ORC if specialized tools (Presto, Hive) are primary users
explain: The deciding factor between Parquet and ORC is the query engine ecosystem. Parquet has broader AWS support (Athena, Glue, Redshift Spectrum, EMR Spark) making it the default choice for most data lakes. ORC is preferred when Hive or Presto are the primary query engines, as ORC was originally optimized for Hive's execution engine. For archival with no specific primary use case, Parquet's broader compatibility minimizes future migration risk.

q: A Glue Crawler runs nightly on s3://data/year=YYYY/month=MM/day=DD/. New partitions arrive before crawler. After 10 days, how many partitions does crawler process?
options: Run the Glue Crawler over all historical partitions on every scheduled execution
10 (all new partitions); incremental mode avoids re-scanning old
Variable partition count depending on how many files S3 event notifications trigger
1 (new partitions only)
explain: In Glue Crawler incremental mode ('Crawl new folders only'), the crawler records the S3 prefixes already cataloged and on subsequent runs only lists and samples new prefixes since the last run. After 10 days with one new partition per day, the next incremental crawl processes only the 10 new day-partitions rather than all historical partitions. This reduces crawler runtime from hours to minutes as the dataset grows.

q: A data lake stores 1 PB time-series data with daily partitions. Query patterns: 80% on last 7 days; 15% on last 90 days; 5% full scan. Costs dominated by storage. What tiering minimizes cost?
options: <7 days Standard; 7-90 days Standard-IA; >90 days Glacier/Deep Archive
Keep all Standard
Single-region replication
Move all data lake objects to Glacier immediately after ingestion to minimize storage costs
explain: Tiered S3 storage based on access frequency dramatically reduces data lake costs at petabyte scale. Data accessed within 7 days (hot queries) stays in Standard for lowest retrieval latency. Data accessed weekly to monthly transitions to Standard-IA (40% cheaper) after 7 days with acceptable millisecond retrieval. Data older than 90 days accessed less than once per year transitions to Glacier or Deep Archive (80-95% cheaper) with acceptable minutes-to-hours retrieval latency.

q: A Redshift cluster (dc2.large, 5 nodes) has distribution key (cust_id). Queries filter by region (not in key). Explain plans show huge scans. What is the optimization?
options: Create GSI on region; or add region to sort key
Increase node count
Add region column to distribution key
No optimization
explain: Adding region to the Redshift SORTKEY enables zone map-based block skipping during table scans — Redshift skips 1 MB disk blocks where the zone map's min/max range for region does not include the filter value. The distribution key (cust_id) determines data placement for join optimization; it does not affect filter performance. Increasing node count adds parallelism but does not reduce data scanned per query if zone maps cannot prune blocks.

q: A team implements cross-region replication: us-east-1 to us-west-2. Source uses KMS key in us-east-1. Replicated objects become corrupted when source key is deleted. How to prevent this?
options: Never delete source key
No encryption in transit
Single key for all regions
Use separate KMS keys per region; replicate with destination key re-encryption
explain: S3 cross-region replication with re-encryption at the destination uses a separate KMS key in us-west-2 to re-encrypt replicated objects. This means the destination objects are protected by the us-west-2 key — even if the us-east-1 source key is deleted, the replicated objects remain accessible via the destination key. Using a single cross-region KMS key creates a dependency where deleting the key corrupts both source and replicated data.

q: A DynamoDB table uses partition key (user_id), sort key (timestamp). High-cardinality IDs (1M+) with uneven distribution cause hot partitions. What optimization applies?
options: Implement DAX caching layer for hot keys; consider application-level deduplication
Increase provisioned capacity globally
Migrate from DynamoDB to RDS for all data lake metadata storage requirements
Add more sort keys
explain: Uneven user_id distribution (some popular users generate far more requests) creates hot partitions where a small number of DynamoDB partition keys receive disproportionate traffic. DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) caches frequently accessed items in-memory with microsecond read latency, absorbing hot-key reads before they hit DynamoDB partition capacity limits. Application-level deduplication can also reduce redundant reads. Global RCU increases help uniform traffic but not hot-partition bottlenecks.

q: A Lake Formation data lake has 100 tables. Different teams own subsets. How should permissions scale to 500 tables without manual configuration per table?
options: Spreadsheet tracking
Lake Formation database-level permissions + data tags for attribute-based access control
S3 bucket policies per table
Per-table IAM roles
explain: AWS Lake Formation attribute-based access control (ABAC) using LF-Tags scales to any number of tables without per-table configuration. Data stewards assign ownership tags (e.g., team=sales, team=finance) to tables. IAM roles are granted access to resources matching their team's LF-tag — new tables automatically inherit permissions when tagged correctly. Per-table IAM roles or S3 bucket policies require O(tables×teams) manual configuration that becomes unmanageable at 500 tables.

q: A Redshift Spectrum query on 50 TB S3 Parquet files takes 10 minutes. Equivalent Redshift table query: 2 minutes. What optimization is most impactful?
options: Disable Spectrum
Copy all to Redshift
Use Spectrum for all
Improve S3 partitioning for pruning; pre-aggregate frequent queries to Redshift
explain: Redshift Spectrum's 5× query latency vs native Redshift is primarily due to insufficient S3 partition pruning — Spectrum scans far more data than a native Redshift table scan. Better S3 partitioning (matching query filter columns) enables Spectrum to skip irrelevant partitions, dramatically reducing bytes scanned. For the most frequent aggregation queries, pre-computing and storing results in Redshift native tables eliminates Spectrum overhead entirely while keeping historical data in low-cost S3.

q: A team implements Iceberg on S3 for UPSERT operations. Table has 100M small files; 20 GB of delete markers. What optimization is critical?
options: Incremental only
Bin-packing compaction to merge files; delete file consolidation for stale deletes
Rewrite entire table
No optimization
explain: Apache Iceberg bin-packing compaction merges many small data files into fewer optimally-sized files (targeting 128MB–512MB), reducing Athena query overhead from opening millions of files. Delete file consolidation rewrites data files to apply pending deletes physically, eliminating the merge overhead of reading data + 20 GB of delete markers on every query. Rewriting the entire table at once is expensive and creates downtime; bin-packing processes files incrementally.

q: A DynamoDB table uses on-demand billing with peak WCU spikes. Partition key: user_id; sort key: timestamp. Read throttling: 50%. What is the likely cause?
options: Redesign all DynamoDB tables to use composite sort keys for every access pattern
The Athena cost controls are misconfigured and will not enforce spending limits
Hot partition from skewed distribution; DAX caching + query optimization
Insufficient global throughput
explain: DynamoDB on-demand mode scales automatically but starts from a warm-up baseline. If write traffic spikes dramatically (e.g., a sudden batch load 10× previous peak), DynamoDB throttles requests during the capacity ramp-up period. For skewed user_id distributions where a small set of hot users drives most WCU consumption, DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) caches read responses and reduces the effective WCU demand. Query optimization to avoid unnecessary reads further reduces pressure on hot partitions.

q: A Redshift cluster (10 nodes) has distribution key (customer_id, date). Query latency: 5 sec baseline; 30 sec with concurrency. Concurrency Scaling not enabled. What is primary factor?
options: Storage limit reached on compute nodes, causing spill-to-disk and high latency
CPU saturation on the leader node from query parsing and result set compilation
Network bandwidth saturation between Redshift nodes during data shuffling
Query queuing from concurrency; enable Concurrency Scaling for burst capacity
explain: The 5-second baseline query latency growing to 5 minutes under 10 concurrent queries is a classic WLM queue saturation pattern — queries wait in queue rather than slowing down during execution. Without Concurrency Scaling enabled, Redshift's WLM can only run as many queries simultaneously as there are queue slots. Enabling Concurrency Scaling automatically adds burst cluster capacity, allowing queued queries to execute immediately on additional compute without permanently increasing the cluster size.

q: A COPY command loads 100 GB from S3 manifest (50 files). One file is corrupted. Load partially succeeds (95%). Is data in Redshift?
options: Deleting an occupied S3 bucket requires a force-delete flag that bypasses object protection
Partial load OK
Yes, 95% loaded
No, COPY is atomic; entire transaction rolled back on any error
explain: Amazon Redshift COPY operations are fully atomic — the entire load either succeeds or fails as a single transaction. If any file in the manifest is corrupted or causes an error, Redshift rolls back the entire COPY transaction, leaving no data loaded. This atomicity guarantees data consistency: there will never be a partially loaded dataset from a COPY operation. The engineer must fix the corrupted file and re-run the COPY with the corrected manifest.

q: A DynamoDB Global Table replicates us-east-1 to us-west-2. A write succeeds in us-east-1 but read in us-west-2 shows old data momentarily. Is this a bug?
options: Always consistent
The replication is broken — cross-region data shares do not support column filters
No, Global Tables provide eventual consistency; region-local reads are strongly consistent
Never consistent
explain: DynamoDB Global Tables provide eventual consistency for cross-region replication — writes in us-east-1 are asynchronously replicated to us-west-2, with typical lag of 1-5 seconds. Within the originating region, reads are strongly consistent if using ConsistentRead=true. Applications requiring cross-region strong consistency must implement application-level coordination, as Global Tables cannot provide synchronous cross-region writes without prohibitive latency.

q: A S3 lifecycle rule transitions: Standard -> Standard-IA (30 days) -> Glacier (90 days) -> delete (2 years). Query on 6-month-old data costs more than 6-week-old. Why?
options: Query cost in Standard-IA and Glacier is identical to Standard since S3 handles retrieval
Network egress cost between AWS regions for cross-region Glacier retrieval operations
Glacier retrieval cost (per GB) vs Standard access; query requires restore
There is no cost difference between storage tiers since retrieval fees are waived for Glue
explain: Six-month-old data has been transitioned to S3 Glacier (after 90 days), which charges a per-GB retrieval fee and requires a restore operation (taking minutes to hours) before the data can be queried. Six-week-old data sits in S3 Standard-IA, which has lower retrieval costs and allows immediate byte-range reads without restoration. AWS Athena cannot query Glacier objects directly — they must be restored to S3 Standard or Standard-IA first, adding both latency and cost. The main distractor about cross-region egress is wrong because lifecycle transitions within the same region do not incur inter-region data transfer fees.

q: An Aurora cluster has 1 primary + 20 read replicas. An analytical query locks tables for 10 min. OLTP queries experience <1ms SLA violations. What architecture fixes this?
options: Single cluster only
Add more replicas
Increase resources
Separate Aurora cluster for analytics; OLTP replicas isolated from locking
explain: Even with 20 read replicas, all replicas share the same Aurora cluster storage and are subject to table-level locking from analytical queries running on any replica in the cluster. The correct fix is to create a separate Aurora cluster (or use Redshift/Athena) dedicated to analytics, completely isolating those long-running locking queries from the OLTP read replicas. Simply adding more replicas does not resolve lock contention because the lock is at the storage layer. The separate cluster approach ensures that analytical workloads never impact OLTP latency regardless of query complexity.

q: An Aurora PostgreSQL read replica is promoted to primary. What happens to application connections?
options: Existing connections automatically transfer to the promoted primary with zero interruption
Application must update endpoint to new primary; existing connections drop
No application change is needed since Aurora automatically updates the cluster endpoint DNS
Manual intervention by the DBA to update each application connection string individually
explain: When an Aurora PostgreSQL read replica is manually promoted to become a new primary cluster, it gets a new cluster endpoint that differs from the original primary. Applications using the original primary endpoint will get connection refused errors after promotion completes. Applications must update their connection strings to point to the promoted cluster endpoint. This is different from Aurora's automatic failover, where the cluster DNS endpoint automatically updates within 30 seconds.

q: A data lake uses S3 Intelligent-Tiering. Recent data (30 days) is accessed frequently. Older data accessed rarely. What tiering happens automatically?
options: Manually move S3 objects to Glacier using the AWS console when storage costs exceed budget
Store all data lake objects in the same S3 storage class regardless of access patterns
Recent -> Standard (frequent tier); older -> Archive (infrequent tier)
Keep all data permanently in S3 Standard regardless of access frequency or age
explain: S3 Intelligent-Tiering monitors object-level access patterns and automatically moves objects between the Frequent Access tier (equivalent cost to S3 Standard) and the Infrequent Access tier (40% cheaper) based on access history. Recent data (30 days) with frequent access stays in the Frequent tier for low-latency retrieval. Older data with rare access transitions to the Infrequent tier automatically after 30 days of no access — without retrieval fees or performance impact.

q: A Step Functions pipeline (Extract -> Transform -> Load) fails at Load. Retry policy: 3 attempts, exponential backoff. On final failure, entire pipeline restarts. How should error handling be fixed?
options: No error handling
Increase retry limit
Disable retry logic in Step Functions to prevent duplicate processing of failed stages
Catch Load failure; route to DLQ for manual investigation; prevent cascade
explain: A Catch block in the Step Functions Load state that routes failures to a dead-letter queue (SQS DLQ) enables independent error handling: the DLQ holds failed load payloads for manual investigation and replay, while the pipeline state machine terminates cleanly without cascading failure to upstream states. Without this pattern, a persistent load failure causes the entire pipeline to remain blocked until manual intervention.

q: An MWAA DAG orchestrates 50 daily tasks. DAG parsing: 2 sec; execution: 5 min. Airflow UI feels slow. What is likely cause and fix?
options: Database slowness is the only cause of Kinesis consumer lag during peak processing
Upgrade Airflow version
Heavy DAG definition causes parsing overhead; implement serialization cache or split DAG
Scheduler overloaded
explain: Apache Airflow parses all DAG files on every scheduler heartbeat. A DAG with 50 tasks featuring complex Python imports or dynamic generation incurs 2-second parse overhead per cycle. Enabling DAG serialization stores parsed DAG structure in the metadata database so the webserver reads cached JSON rather than re-parsing Python on each page load — dramatically improving UI responsiveness. Splitting a large DAG into smaller DAGs also reduces per-DAG parse time.

q: An EventBridge rule triggers Lambda on S3 PUT events. Lambda processes 100 KB files at 10,000 files/hour (peak). Lambda timeout: 5 minutes. What scalability issue exists?
options: Cold starts causing timeouts
S3 bandwidth limit
EventBridge limits
Reserve Lambda concurrency; implement container reuse; consider async processing
explain: Lambda cold starts add 500ms to 2+ seconds of initialization latency for the first invocation on each new container instance. For an S3-triggered pipeline processing 100 KB files where business SLA requires < 200ms processing time, cold starts breach the SLA on every new container spin-up. Reserved concurrency pre-allocates containers and combined with container reuse (initializing SDK clients in global scope) keeps warm containers available for subsequent invocations.

q: A CloudWatch alarm "Glue job duration > 1 hour" fires for 20% of jobs. Investigation: only 5% truly slow; 15% false positives during off-peak. How should alerting be tuned?
options: Manual monitoring
Use CloudWatch anomaly detection to learn baselines; suppress during known maintenance
Remove the CloudWatch alarm and replace it with manual daily log reviews
Increase threshold to 2 hours
explain: CloudWatch anomaly detection applies ML to learn the normal baseline for a metric (e.g., Glue job duration varies by time of day and day of week). Alarms are triggered only when the actual value deviates significantly from the predicted baseline — eliminating 15% false positives that occur because off-peak jobs run with different resource contention. Alarm suppression during known maintenance windows prevents additional noise. Simply raising the threshold to 2 hours would miss the 5% of genuinely slow jobs.

q: A Kinesis consumer has lag 5 minutes; threshold is 2 minutes. Consumer processes 100 MB/min at max concurrency. What is the bottleneck?
options: Consumer throughput insufficient; scale workers or optimize processing; check downstream throttling
Network bandwidth
Increase shard count
Kinesis shard count too low
explain: When Kinesis consumer lag exceeds the SLA threshold and the consumer is already at maximum concurrency processing 100 MB/min, the bottleneck is either insufficient processing throughput or downstream write throttling. Solutions: scale workers horizontally (more Lambda concurrency or larger ECS tasks), optimize per-record processing time, or investigate downstream throttling (DynamoDB WCU limits, RDS connection pool exhaustion) that is slowing write operations and causing backpressure.

q: A pipeline SLA: "data available by 8 AM daily". Pipeline runs 11 PM - 6 AM. Current lag: 30 minutes. Should this trigger an alert?
options: No monitoring needed
Set CloudWatch alarm thresholds at random intervals to detect unexpected traffic patterns
Yes, always alert if lag exists
No, SLA is met; alert only if lag > threshold (2 hours)
explain: Data freshness SLA monitoring should alert based on SLA breach risk, not the mere existence of lag. With data available by 8 AM and the pipeline completing at ~6:30 AM (30-minute lag added to 6 AM completion), the SLA is comfortably met with 1.5 hours of buffer. A CloudWatch alarm should fire only when lag exceeds the threshold where SLA breach becomes imminent — for a 2-hour buffer, alerting at > 1.5 hours of lag is appropriate.

q: A Glue job bookmark tracks state by partition key. File s3://data/partition=X/file.csv is re-uploaded with new data. What happens?
options: Bookmark detects timestamp change; file marked for reprocessing
Manual reprocessing
Bookmark ignores
Bookmark skips; file not reprocessed
explain: AWS Glue job bookmarks track processed S3 files using a combination of S3 key (path) and object modification timestamp. When a file at the same path (partition=X/file.csv) is overwritten with new content, the S3 object's LastModified timestamp changes. Glue's bookmark detects this timestamp change and marks the file for reprocessing in the next run. If the file content were unchanged (same timestamp), the bookmark would skip it.

q: A Lambda function: receive order message, upsert DynamoDB with order_id as key. Duplicate message arrives. What guarantees exist?
options: Use at-most-once delivery semantics, accepting potential data loss for simpler implementation
Accept at-most-once delivery and tolerate data loss for non-critical metrics
At-least-once
Upsert with same key = idempotent; duplicate results in single update (exactly-once)
explain: DynamoDB upsert operations (PutItem with the same primary key) are inherently idempotent — submitting the same order_id multiple times results in the same final state (the latest version of the item). This means Lambda can safely process duplicate SQS messages without creating duplicate records, achieving exactly-once semantics at the data layer even with at-least-once message delivery from SQS.

q: A DMS replication task shows: Full Load 100%, CDC Latency 2 sec, Validation Errors 50. Should cutover proceed?
options: No validation needed
Yes, full load done
No, investigate validation errors before cutover; CDC latency acceptable but errors indicate discrepancies
CDC latency only
explain: DMS validation errors (50 discrepancies) indicate rows that exist in the source but are missing or different in the target. Proceeding to cutover with 50 validation errors means users will immediately encounter missing or incorrect records in the target database. A 2-second CDC latency is acceptable (typically resolves within seconds after cutover). The validation errors must be root-caused (corrupted rows, unsupported data types, constraint violations) and resolved before the cutover window.

q: A Step Functions pipeline has sequential stages causing bottleneck. Extract (2 min), Transform (5 min), Enrich (3 min), Load (1 min). Can these be parallelized?
options: No parallelization
Sequential only
Run all pipeline stages in parallel regardless of data dependencies between stages
Enrich can be parallel with Transform if they do not depend on each other
explain: AWS Step Functions Map states execute multiple iterations in parallel when the iterations are independent. In an ETL pipeline with Extract → Enrich → Transform → Load stages, if Enrich and Transform do not have a data dependency (Enrich does not need Transform's output and vice versa), they can be placed in a Parallel state that runs both branches simultaneously, reducing total pipeline duration from the sum of all stage durations to the duration of the longest parallel branch.

q: An EventBridge rule triggers Glue job on S3 PUT. During high-volume uploads, Glue jobs queue. How should concurrency be managed?
options: Drop events that arrive during peak traffic periods to prevent consumer lag
Sequential processing only
EventBridge rate limit + SQS batching; Glue parallel job execution
Kinesis has no per-shard throughput limits and scales automatically to any ingest rate
explain: When high-volume S3 uploads trigger EventBridge rules that launch Glue jobs directly, the number of simultaneous Glue job runs can exceed Glue's concurrency limits, causing jobs to queue or be throttled. The correct pattern is to route EventBridge events through SQS, which acts as a buffer and allows Glue to consume batches at a controlled rate matching its concurrent job capacity. Dropping events during peak traffic would result in data loss, which is never acceptable in a data engineering pipeline. Sequential processing would eliminate the throughput advantage of parallel Glue execution.

q: A pipeline monitoring shows: Duration 2h (was 1h), Error rate 0.1%, Freshness lag 3h (SLA 2h). What should be prioritized?
options: Lower the data freshness SLA to eliminate the need for real-time pipeline monitoring
Ignore 0.1% errors
Fix error rate first
Investigate duration increase (causing lag); then address lag
explain: The pipeline's data freshness lag of 3 hours exceeds the 2-hour SLA, making it the highest-priority business impact to address. The duration increase from 1h to 2h is the root cause of the freshness lag — as the pipeline takes longer to run, data arrives later downstream. The 0.1% error rate, while worth tracking, is not causing the SLA breach and should be addressed after the latency issue. Fixing the duration regression (e.g., checking for data volume spikes, executor bottlenecks, or resource contention) will directly reduce the freshness lag and restore SLA compliance.

q: An MWAA DAG with 10 tasks shows 2% sporadic failures. Retries eventually succeed. What Airflow configuration improves reliability?
options: Increase retry count with exponential backoff; enable detailed logging
Disable alerting
Increase DAG timeout
Disable retry logic entirely to prevent any possibility of duplicate record processing
explain: Increasing the Airflow task retry count with exponential backoff (retries=3, retry_delay=timedelta(seconds=30), retry_exponential_backoff=True) handles transient failures — network timeouts, API throttling, transient database errors — without requiring human intervention. Detailed logging with task-level log capture preserves visibility into what caused each failure for post-incident analysis. Increasing DAG timeout does not improve task-level resilience.

q: A team processes both batch (nightly load) and streaming (real-time API calls). Should data be unified or separate?
options: Unified data lake; route batch to S3/Redshift, stream to Kinesis, consolidate via ETL
All data processing workloads should use streaming regardless of latency requirements
Manual consolidation
Always separate
explain: A unified data lake architecture routes batch data to S3 (and optionally Redshift for analytics) and streaming data to Kinesis, then uses ETL (Glue or Kinesis Firehose) to consolidate both into a single queryable layer. This approach avoids data duplication across separate systems while still using the optimal ingestion path for each workload type. Forcing all workloads to use streaming regardless of latency requirements adds unnecessary complexity and cost for batch use cases that have no real-time requirement. Always separating batch and stream into entirely disconnected systems makes unified reporting and joins across workloads difficult.

q: A Lake Formation table contains customer records. Column "ssn" has fine-grained access control: hidden from non-admin users. Analyst runs "SELECT * FROM table". What columns are returned?
options: The SSN field is masked automatically by DynamoDB before replication to the replica region
Return a generic error code and halt all downstream processing until manual intervention
All columns except ssn (filtered by Lake Formation policy)
Grant access to all columns in the table and rely on application-level filtering
explain: AWS Lake Formation column-level security enforces access control at the query layer — when an analyst runs SELECT *, Lake Formation intercepts the query and strips out any columns they are not authorized to see before returning results. The analyst receives all permitted columns but the SSN column is silently omitted without generating an error, which is the standard behavior for column-level access control.
