This enforcement happens transparently regardless of whether the analyst uses SELECT * or explicitly lists column names. Application-level filtering is not a substitute because it relies on developers correctly implementing controls in every application, which is error-prone and not auditable.

q: A data lake uses separate KMS keys per dataset (Key-A for Finance, Key-B for Marketing). Key-A is compromised. What is the blast radius?
options: Both keys affected
All datasets exposed
Only Finance data exposed; Marketing data protected by Key-B
The data lake has no access restrictions because all employees are trusted insiders
explain: Separate KMS Customer Managed Keys (CMKs) per data classification (Finance key, Marketing key, HR key) limits the blast radius of a key compromise. If Finance's CMK is exposed, only Finance data can be decrypted; Marketing and HR data encrypted with different CMKs remain protected. Key segmentation also enables fine-grained access control over who can decrypt each dataset without requiring S3 bucket policy changes.

q: A Glue job reads 100 GB from source with 5% PII (SSN, email). Before load to Redshift, PII should be masked. Where is masking best implemented?
options: Enable S3 default encryption on the bucket as the sole PII protection mechanism
Glue job transformation or Redshift view with masking function
Store all sensitive fields in plaintext and restrict access via IAM policies alone
Process all pipeline orchestration manually by running each Glue job step sequentially
explain: Masking PII in the Glue ETL transformation step (before loading to Redshift) ensures sensitive values are never written to the analytical warehouse. Alternatively, Redshift Dynamic Data Masking (DDM) masks PII at query time based on the querying role — authorized analysts see plaintext values while other roles see masked versions. Both approaches prevent unauthorized access but differ in whether PII is stored unmasked in the database.

q: A data lake contains customer PII (100 TB). Encryption at-rest enabled with KMS. Is encryption in transit needed?
options: No, rest encryption sufficient
Only for sensitive columns
Yes; TLS in transit + KMS at rest = defense-in-depth
Application encryption only
explain: Dual-layer encryption — KMS SSE at rest combined with TLS in transit — provides defense-in-depth for a 100 TB customer PII data lake. Encryption at rest protects against unauthorized S3 access (misconfigured bucket policies, AWS account compromise). TLS in transit protects against interception during data transfer between AWS services or to external consumers. Both layers together satisfy GDPR's encryption requirement for personal data.

q: A Kinesis stream contains payment data. Encryption at-rest uses KMS. Requirement: prevent exfiltration to unencrypted endpoints. What additional control is needed?
options: Application encryption
KMS alone sufficient
Apply no access restrictions and rely on network segmentation for data protection
TLS for transit + IAM policies restricting consumers + VPC endpoints
explain: Defense-in-depth for payment data in Kinesis combines: KMS encryption at rest (data unreadable if Kinesis storage is compromised), TLS in transit (data protected during transmission from producers to Kinesis), IAM policies restricting consumer access to authorized roles only (preventing unauthorized reads), and VPC endpoints (keeping traffic off the public internet). Each layer independently protects against a different threat vector.

q: A compliance audit requires: "evidence of all access to PII data". What logging is essential?
options: Manually review CloudWatch logs daily to identify pipeline compliance violations
Store all intermediate state exclusively in S3 without using any in-memory processing
CloudTrail (API access), S3 access logs, Athena query logs, Lake Formation access logs
Rely on application-level error messages without any centralized log aggregation
explain: A comprehensive PII audit trail for compliance requires logging at multiple layers: CloudTrail records all AWS API calls that access S3 objects or query Athena; S3 server access logging captures individual object-level GET and PUT operations; Athena query logs record the full SQL of each query including the querying IAM principal; Lake Formation access logs track fine-grained table and column access events. Together these provide irrefutable evidence for auditors.

q: A compliance requirement: "delete customer data 90 days after account closure". How should deletion be automated?
options: S3 lifecycle rules + DynamoDB TTL; tag for closure; automate deletion
Retain all data lake objects indefinitely without any lifecycle or expiry policies
Infinite retention
Write a custom shell script to implement the data quality validation logic manually
explain: S3 lifecycle rules and DynamoDB TTL are the purpose-built AWS mechanisms for automating data expiry without manual intervention. When a customer account is closed, the closure timestamp is tagged on the S3 objects and DynamoDB items; lifecycle rules and TTL policies then automatically delete the data after 90 days, ensuring compliance with regulatory data retention requirements. Manual deletion scripts are error-prone, do not scale, and cannot reliably guarantee the 90-day SLA across all data stores. Retaining data indefinitely violates the compliance requirement and exposes the organization to regulatory penalties.

q: A database contains PII. IAM policies grant: "s3:GetObject on arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*". Is this least privilege?
options: Grant wildcard IAM permissions to all Glue job roles to simplify permission management
Cross-account Lake Formation shares automatically deny all column access by default
No, should specify specific objects/prefixes; tag sensitive data separately
All streaming records are guaranteed to arrive exactly once with no duplicates by default
explain: The IAM policy grants s3:GetObject on arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/* which allows reading every object in the bucket, including objects in prefixes or folders that contain non-PII data or data the role should not access. Least-privilege principle requires scoping the resource ARN to specific prefixes containing only the objects the Glue job actually needs (e.g., arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/pii-data/). Additionally, PII columns should be tagged in AWS Glue Data Catalog and protected via Lake Formation column-level security so that even if the bucket policy is misconfigured, column access remains controlled. Granting wildcard permissions to simplify management is an anti-pattern that violates both least privilege and GDPR/HIPAA data minimization requirements.

q: A Lake Formation data share created from Account A (owner) to Account B (consumer). Account B user queries shared table. What permissions are checked?
options: Manual approval
Lake Formation automatically grants all necessary permissions without explicit configuration
S3 bucket policy in Account A
Lake Formation permission in Account A + IAM role in Account B
explain: AWS Lake Formation cross-account data sharing requires configuration in both accounts. The resource owner (Account A) grants Lake Formation permissions to the consumer account (Account B) or a specific IAM role in Account B. The consumer account must also configure an IAM role with permission to assume the cross-account resource access and with the necessary Lake Formation read permissions. Both halves of the configuration are required — Lake Formation grant alone is insufficient.

q: A Secrets Manager credential for RDS password is rotated every 90 days. Glue job assumes IAM role with GetSecret permission. Does rotation require job changes?
options: Job must restart
No, Secrets Manager handles rotation transparently; IAM role persists access
Yes, manual update
Secrets not rotatable
explain: AWS Secrets Manager handles automatic credential rotation transparently — when the RDS password rotates, Secrets Manager updates both the secret value and the RDS user password simultaneously, so any application that retrieves the secret value via the Secrets Manager API always gets the current valid password. The Glue job's IAM role with secretsmanager:GetSecretValue permission ensures it can always fetch the latest credential without any code changes or job restarts. The rotation process is atomic and maintains application continuity. The distractor suggesting jobs must restart is wrong because Glue jobs fetch the secret at runtime on each execution, so they naturally pick up the rotated value.

q: A company processes EU customer data (GDPR) and US data (no residency). EU data must not leave EU. How should infrastructure be designed?
options: Use a single IAM role with full data lake access for all pipeline components
Single global region
Separate data lakes per region; no cross-region replication for EU data
Replicate EU to US
explain: GDPR requires that EU personal data not leave EU territory without adequate protection mechanisms (adequacy decision or SCCs). Maintaining separate data lakes — an EU lake in an EU region (Frankfurt, Ireland) and a US lake in a US region — with no cross-region replication for EU data ensures residency compliance. Attempting to process EU data in a US region violates GDPR data residency requirements regardless of encryption.

q: A team implements SOX compliance for Redshift warehouse. What controls are essential?
options: Encryption, WLM for segregation, MFA for admin, audit logging, change tracking
Enable daily S3 backups as the sole compliance mechanism for audit log retention
Skip governance controls to reduce pipeline complexity and improve development velocity
Password policies
explain: SOX compliance for Redshift requires: encryption at rest (KMS) and in transit (TLS) for data integrity; workload management (WLM) queue isolation between finance teams and IT operations (segregation of duties); MFA for admin accounts to prevent unauthorized schema changes; comprehensive audit logging (CloudTrail, Redshift audit logs) for access and query tracking; and change management controls (no direct production schema changes without approval).

q: A financial firm requires immutable audit logs (WORM - write-once-read-many). What S3 feature applies?
options: Versioning only
Standard encryption
Enable DEBUG-level logging on every Lambda invocation to capture full payload content
S3 Object Lock in GOVERNANCE or COMPLIANCE mode
explain: S3 Object Lock implements WORM (Write Once Read Many) storage by preventing objects from being deleted or overwritten for a defined retention period. COMPLIANCE mode is the strongest setting — no user, including the root account or AWS Support, can delete or modify a locked object before the retention period expires, making it ideal for financial audit logs subject to SEC or FINRA regulations. GOVERNANCE mode also prevents accidental deletion but allows privileged IAM users with s3:BypassGovernanceRetention to override the lock, which may not satisfy strict compliance mandates. Versioning alone does not prevent deletion — a user can delete all versions including delete markers.

q: A Lake Formation setup has tables: public (logs), confidential (financials), restricted (PII). How should permissions reflect classification?
options: No classification
All tables same access
Lake Formation tags per classification; restrict access by tag; use row/column filters
Manual spreadsheet
explain: AWS Lake Formation attribute-based access control (ABAC) using LF-Tags assigns classification tags (e.g., sensitivity=confidential, sensitivity=restricted) to databases and tables. IAM roles are granted access based on tag values rather than explicit table lists, meaning new tables automatically inherit access control from their tags without requiring policy updates. This scales to hundreds of tables without manual permission management for each new dataset.

q: An Athena workgroup has max query cost of $50. Team member runs expensive query ($100). What governance workflow applies?
options: The query automatically succeeds with a warning logged to CloudTrail for review
Query blocked; escalation required; approval enables override with cost acknowledgment
The workgroup permanently denies all future queries from the user without possibility of override
No cost controls are enforced since workgroup limits only apply to data scanned, not cost
explain: Athena workgroup cost controls enforce a maximum bytes-scanned or cost limit per query, and when a query exceeds that limit, the query is cancelled before it completes. The correct governance workflow requires an escalation path: the user is informed why the query was blocked, and an authorized approver (data platform team or manager) can temporarily override the workgroup limit or help the user optimize their query to run within budget. Permanently denying all future queries from the user without recourse would block legitimate work and is not the intended behavior. Allowing the query to succeed silently with a warning defeats the purpose of the cost control guardrail.

q: A Kinesis stream receives 5,000 RPS. Sharding strategy for 10x growth?
options: Enable automatic schema inference on every read, re-scanning the full dataset each time
Set a fixed batch size of 100 records per Lambda invocation regardless of record payload size
Calculate (5K/1K=5)+buffer; resharding for growth
Use a single Kinesis shard for all IoT telemetry to maintain strict ordering across all devices
explain: Kinesis shard capacity math: each shard supports 1,000 records/second write throughput and 1 MB/s write bandwidth. At 5,000 RPS, the minimum shard count is 5,000/1,000 = 5 shards. For 10x growth (50,000 RPS), the minimum becomes 50 shards. Adding a buffer (typically 20-30%) for traffic spikes means provisioning 60-65 shards. Kinesis supports resharding (splitting shards) to scale capacity without stream recreation.

q: Kinesis consumer lag during peak. Enhanced fan-out enabled. DynamoDB writes (100ms). Bottleneck?
options: Enhanced fan-out subscription limits preventing additional consumers from registering on the shard
The Kinesis shard count being insufficient to distribute records to all enhanced fan-out consumers
Network saturation between the Kinesis consumer and the DynamoDB VPC endpoint
Consumer app (DynamoDB); scale or optimize
explain: Enhanced fan-out eliminates the Kinesis read-side bottleneck by giving each registered consumer a dedicated 2 MB/s per-shard read throughput. If enhanced fan-out is already enabled and consumer lag persists, the bottleneck has moved downstream — to the DynamoDB write throughput (100 writes/second against provisioned capacity) rather than the Kinesis read path. The fix is to scale DynamoDB write capacity or switch to DynamoDB On-Demand mode.

q: Data lake 100 TB historical. Rare queries (<1/year). All Standard. Strategy?
options: Keep all data in S3 Standard and rely on intelligent-tiering to manage access frequency automatically
Deep Archive ($0.004/GB); Expedited for rare
Move all data to S3 Standard-IA immediately to reduce storage cost by 40% for infrequently accessed files
Delete historical data older than 2 years since rare queries can be satisfied from recent data alone
explain: S3 Glacier Deep Archive ($0.00099/GB/month) is the optimal storage class for data accessed less than once per year, providing 99.99% durability at a fraction of S3 Standard cost ($0.023/GB/month). Expedited retrieval (1-5 minutes) is available for occasional access needs at a per-retrieval fee. Moving 100 TB from Standard to Deep Archive saves approximately $2,200/month ($2,300 vs. $100) — over $26,000/year.

q: Redshift query: 30 sec baseline, 10 concurrent=5 min. Cause?
options: Queuing; allocate slots or Scaling
Raw S3 storage cost, which is negligible compared to the operational overhead
Measure net new records ingested per hour as the sole pipeline performance indicator
Increase CPU allocation for all Glue workers regardless of the actual bottleneck type
explain: Redshift query queuing occurs when concurrent queries exceed the allocated WLM (Workload Management) queue slots. At 10 concurrent queries sharing 5 queue slots, 5 queries must wait, causing 5-minute response times versus the 30-second baseline. Solutions: increase WLM queue slots (reduces concurrency protection), enable Concurrency Scaling (automatically adds cluster capacity during bursts), or implement query routing to separate queues by query type.

q: Redshift join: dim 100 MB (ALL) + fact 500 GB (KEY). 5 sec baseline, 10 concurrent=30 sec. Cause?
options: Reduce dimension table cardinality by merging all slowly-changing attributes into a single column
Use a fact table in Redshift with all dimension attributes denormalized into a single wide table
No transformation is required since source and target schemas are always identical
Queuing; Concurrency Scaling
explain: When the bottleneck in Redshift is query queuing rather than query execution time, distributing dimension tables as ALL distribution (full copies on every node) and collocating fact tables by KEY distribution resolves data movement overhead — but queuing overhead dominates when 10 concurrent queries compete for 5 WLM slots. Concurrency Scaling adds burst cluster capacity to handle peak concurrency without permanently increasing cluster size.

q: DynamoDB session TTL 24 hrs. Inactive 30 hrs, return. Result?
options: Delete; re-auth
Retain all historical raw records indefinitely in S3 without applying any lifecycle policies
Extend the Kinesis stream shard count by 10x to absorb all late-arriving records
Restore the previous DynamoDB table snapshot to roll back all recent schema changes
explain: DynamoDB TTL marks items for deletion after the specified Unix timestamp in the TTL attribute. Items with a 24-hour TTL are deleted within 48 hours after expiry (DynamoDB processes TTL deletions asynchronously). A user whose session TTL expired 30 hours ago will find their session item deleted and must re-authenticate to create a new session record.

q: Global Table across 3 regions. Write us-east-1; replicate us-west-2 (200ms), eu (300ms). App latency?
options: Aggregate all numeric fields using SUM before writing to the target to reduce output record count
1-5ms (async); sees primary only
Set a 300-second timeout on all Glue ETL jobs to prevent long-running transforms
Configure a 200-millisecond polling interval on the Kinesis stream consumer
explain: DynamoDB Global Tables use asynchronous replication with typical cross-region lag of 1-5ms to seconds depending on distance. A write confirmed in us-east-1 is replicated asynchronously to us-west-2 and eu-west-1. A read from us-west-2 immediately after the us-east-1 write may return the pre-write value if replication has not completed. The strongly consistent read option only applies within the local region.

q: Step Functions: Validate->Process->Notify. Validate succeeds empty. Process unnecessary. Prevent?
options: Choice; skip if empty
No transformation is required since source and target schemas are always identical
Buffer all streaming records for 24 hours before any processing to ensure completeness
Manually inspect each record for schema compliance before writing to the target
explain: AWS Step Functions Choice states evaluate condition expressions (e.g., IsPresent, NumericGreaterThan, StringEquals) on the state input JSON to route execution to different state paths. A Choice state checking whether the validation output contains records can route to Process when records exist and skip directly to End (or a notification state) when the dataset is empty — avoiding unnecessary Processing Lambda invocations.

q: MWAA 100 tasks, 1% fail, 3 retries exponential. Cost impact?
options: Double the number of Kinesis shards every time IncomingBytes exceeds 50% of current capacity
No transformation is required since source and target schemas are always identical
3% extra; backoff needed
Set all DPU workers to minimum capacity and rely on auto-scaling to add resources dynamically
explain: With 100 Airflow tasks running and 1% failure rate (1 task/run), 3 retries with exponential backoff add approximately 3% extra task execution cost in compute time. More importantly, exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s delays) prevents retry storms — if all 100 tasks fail simultaneously (e.g., upstream database outage), exponential backoff ensures retries are staggered rather than simultaneously overwhelming the recovering system.

q: Lambda consumer: 100 KB batch, 128 MB memory, 10ms, 1000 concurrent. Pressure?
options: Set all DPU workers to minimum capacity and rely on auto-scaling to add resources dynamically
1000×128MB=128GB limit; hitting ceiling
No transformation is required since source and target schemas are always identical
Limit each Kinesis record to 100KB and drop any record exceeding this size silently
explain: Lambda concurrent memory consumption: 1,000 concurrent executions × 128 MB each = 128 GB of total memory across the account. AWS Lambda enforces per-account and per-region memory limits. If the account is running other Lambda workloads, 128 GB of concurrent memory consumption may approach or exceed the account-level Lambda memory quota, causing invocation throttling and 429 TooManyRequests errors.

q: Lake Formation DB shared cross-account. Column security in A. B queries. See masked?
options: No; filters apply via share
Lake Formation column filters in the source account are not propagated to cross-account resource shares
The destination account can override the source account column filters using its own Lake Formation policies
All streaming records are guaranteed to arrive exactly once with no duplicates by default
explain: AWS Lake Formation data shares propagate the column-level security filters defined in the source account (Account A) to cross-account consumers (Account B). When Account B queries the shared table through Athena, Lake Formation in Account A enforces the column filters — Account B users cannot see columns that Account A's Lake Formation policy restricts, regardless of the IAM permissions in Account B.

q: A data engineer is building a real-time pipeline that ingests clickstream events from a mobile app into Amazon Kinesis Data Streams. The shard count is currently 4 and the producer is experiencing ProvisionedThroughputExceededException errors under peak load. What is the MOST EFFECTIVE fix?
options: Switch from Kinesis to SQS Standard Queue which has unlimited throughput and no shard limit
Increase the Kinesis retention period from 24 hours to 7 days to buffer the overflow
Use the Kinesis Producer Library (KPL) with aggregation enabled — KPL batches multiple records into a single shard write, reducing the number of PUT operations and maximizing per-shard throughput
Enable server-side encryption on the stream — unencrypted streams throttle under high load
explain: KPL aggregation combines multiple small records into a single Kinesis record (up to 1 MB), dramatically reducing the number of PutRecords calls and making full use of each shard's 1 MB/s write throughput. This addresses ProvisionedThroughputExceededException without increasing shard count. Retention period affects data availability, not write throughput. SQS is not a stream and loses ordering guarantees. Encryption has no effect on throughput limits.

q: A Glue ETL job reads a 500 GB Parquet dataset from S3 and joins it with a 10 MB lookup table. The job runs correctly but takes 45 minutes. A data engineer wants to reduce the runtime. Which optimization has the GREATEST impact?
options: Broadcast the 10 MB lookup table using Spark broadcast join — the small table is sent to every worker node, eliminating the expensive shuffle caused by a standard join
Enable Glue job bookmarks to skip previously processed files
Increase the number of Glue workers from 10 to 50 to parallelize the join
Convert the 500 GB dataset from Parquet to CSV for faster schema inference
explain: A broadcast join copies the small 10 MB table to every executor, eliminating the shuffle-intensive sort-merge join that Spark uses by default. Shuffles are the primary cause of long runtimes in distributed joins. Simply adding workers still pays the full shuffle cost. Job bookmarks help with incremental processing, not join performance. Converting from Parquet to CSV would make performance significantly worse.

q: An AWS DMS task migrating a 2 TB PostgreSQL table to Amazon Redshift fails midway through the full-load phase with a 'disk full' error on the replication instance. What is the CORRECT remediation?
options: Increase the replication instance storage or choose a larger instance class — DMS buffers LOB columns and change data on the replication instance disk during migration
Enable DMS multi-AZ which uses a standby replication instance with additional storage
Reduce the DMS table-level batch size to 1 — smaller batches use less memory and no disk space
Switch the migration type from Full Load to CDC-only — CDC does not use local disk storage
explain: DMS uses local disk on the replication instance to stage data during full-load, especially for tables with LOB columns or when buffering CDC events that arrive during the full-load phase. When migrating large tables, the default instance size can be exhausted. The fix is to upgrade to a larger replication instance class or add storage. CDC-only skips the initial full-load entirely, leaving the target empty. Multi-AZ adds failover capability, not storage. Batch size tuning does not control disk usage for LOB staging.

q: A streaming pipeline uses Kinesis Data Firehose to deliver JSON records to Amazon S3. The data science team reports that Athena queries are slow because each S3 prefix contains thousands of tiny files (average 50 KB each). What Firehose configuration change solves this?
options: Enable Firehose server-side encryption — encrypted delivery uses a larger internal buffer
Add a Lambda transformation to the Firehose delivery stream that merges records before writing to S3
Enable Firehose record format conversion to Parquet — format conversion automatically merges small files
Increase the Firehose buffer size to 128 MB and buffer interval to 900 seconds — larger buffers accumulate more records before flushing, producing fewer, larger files
explain: Kinesis Firehose writes to S3 based on two buffering thresholds: buffer size (1–128 MB) and buffer interval (60–900 seconds). Whichever threshold is reached first triggers a flush. Increasing both values means Firehose accumulates more data before writing, producing larger files. At 128 MB / 900s, each S3 object will be substantially larger, dramatically reducing the small-files problem. Format conversion to Parquet improves compression and columnar reads but does not address file size. Lambda transformations process records one-by-one and cannot merge across the buffer boundary.

q: A data engineer runs an AWS Glue crawler on an S3 bucket that contains 3 years of daily Parquet files partitioned by year/month/day. The crawler takes 90 minutes to complete. After the initial run, the partition structure never changes — only new daily partitions are added. How should the engineer reduce crawler runtime going forward?
options: Configure the crawler to use the 'Crawl new folders only' option — after the initial full crawl, the crawler only samples new partitions added since the last run
Schedule the crawler to run hourly instead of daily — more frequent runs process fewer new files per run
Increase the Glue crawler DPU allocation to 10 — more compute units parallelize the S3 listing
Delete and recreate the Glue catalog table daily — fresh catalog entries are faster to populate than incremental updates
explain: Glue crawler incremental mode ('Crawl new folders only') records the S3 paths already cataloged and on subsequent runs only lists and samples paths added since the last crawl. For a dataset where the partition structure is stable and only new date partitions are added daily, this reduces crawler runtime from 90 minutes to seconds or minutes. DPU allocation affects ETL job performance, not crawler speed. Hourly schedules simply run the full slow crawl more often. Deleting the catalog daily defeats the purpose of incremental cataloging.

q: An Apache Kafka topic on Amazon MSK receives 50,000 events per second. A Glue Streaming job consuming this topic shows consistently high lag on 2 of the 8 topic partitions. CPU and memory on the Glue workers are below 30%. What is the MOST LIKELY cause and fix?
options: Glue Streaming does not support MSK — switch to Kinesis Data Streams for Glue Streaming integration
The Glue Streaming job has fewer workers than partitions — Spark assigns one task per partition and with fewer workers some partitions queue
The MSK broker handling those 2 partitions is undersized — increase the MSK broker instance type or rebalance partitions across brokers
The 2 lagging partitions contain larger messages — configure Kafka max.message.bytes to limit message size
explain: In Apache Kafka, each partition is processed by exactly one consumer in a consumer group. If 2 specific partitions show persistent lag while others are healthy, the most likely cause is that the MSK broker responsible for those partition leaders is resource-constrained — an uneven partition distribution (hot broker). Rebalancing partitions across brokers or upgrading the specific broker resolves this. Low CPU/memory on Glue workers rules out worker-side resource exhaustion. Glue Streaming fully supports MSK.

q: A company collects IoT sensor readings via AWS IoT Core and routes them to a Kinesis Data Stream. A downstream Lambda consumer must enrich each reading with device metadata from DynamoDB. Under high load, the Lambda function throttles. What architecture reduces throttling while maintaining ordering per device?
options: Partition the Kinesis stream by device ID and configure Lambda parallelization factor=1 per shard — this limits concurrent Lambda invocations to the shard count and preserves ordering
Batch the DynamoDB lookups inside Lambda using BatchGetItem and increase the Lambda timeout to 5 minutes
Replace Kinesis with SQS FIFO queue — FIFO queues maintain per-device ordering and scale Lambda concurrency automatically
Use Kinesis enhanced fan-out with a dedicated consumer for Lambda — enhanced fan-out provides 2 MB/s per shard per consumer
explain: Kinesis event source mapping for Lambda preserves ordering within a shard by processing records sequentially. Setting parallelization factor=1 means one concurrent Lambda invocation per shard. If throttling occurs due to too many concurrent invocations, ensuring the shard count matches the Lambda concurrency limit controls the rate. The root fix is ensuring the shard count matches the Lambda concurrency limit. Enhanced fan-out helps with read throughput competition but does not prevent Lambda throttling. SQS FIFO loses the enrichment pattern and has different ordering semantics.

q: A data team stores 5 years of financial transaction data in Amazon S3. Data older than 1 year is rarely accessed but must be retained for compliance. Data older than 3 years must be kept for 7 years minimum with immutable storage. Which S3 configuration meets both requirements at lowest cost?
options: Store all data in S3 Standard with no lifecycle policy — compliance requires immediate accessibility at all times
Use S3 Intelligent-Tiering for all data — it automatically moves data to the cheapest tier and supports Object Lock natively

Configure an S3 Lifecycle policy to transition objects to S3 Standard-IA after 1 year, then to S3 Glacier Deep Archive after 3 years. Enable S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode on the Glacier Deep Archive tier with a 7-year retention period
Move data older than 3 years to an on-premises tape library — cloud storage cannot provide legally compliant immutable storage
explain: The two-tier lifecycle policy (Standard-IA after 1 year, Glacier Deep Archive after 3 years) matches the access pattern while minimizing cost. S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode on the archived tier prevents deletion or modification by anyone, including root, for the specified retention period — meeting the 7-year immutability requirement. S3 Intelligent-Tiering does not support Object Lock on archival tiers and has monthly monitoring fees.

q: A data engineer is designing a Redshift schema for a retail analytics workload. The fact table has 2 billion rows and is joined frequently with a 50-row store dimension table and a 500,000-row product dimension table. Which distribution strategy is OPTIMAL?
options: Distribute the fact table on a high-cardinality column like transaction_id — random distribution ensures even data spread

Use ALL distribution for both dimension tables and KEY distribution on the fact table using product_id — this collocates fact and large dimension data while broadcasting the small store table to all slices
Use EVEN distribution for all tables — Redshift's optimizer automatically rebalances data during query execution
Use ALL distribution for the fact table — broadcasting all 2 billion rows ensures every join is local
explain: The optimal Redshift distribution strategy minimizes data movement during joins. The 50-row store dimension uses ALL distribution (trivially small). The 500K-row product dimension also uses ALL distribution given its moderate size. The 2B-row fact table uses KEY distribution on product_id — this collocates fact rows with matching product dimension rows on the same slice, eliminating redistribution for the most expensive join. ALL distribution on a 2B-row table would exhaust every node's disk.

q: An analytics team queries a 10 TB S3 data lake using Amazon Athena. Queries against the last 30 days of data run in seconds, but queries spanning 6-12 months take 8-10 minutes and scan the entire dataset. The data is stored as Parquet partitioned by year/month/day. What change most reduces scan time for historical range queries?
options: Enable Athena result caching — cached query results eliminate re-scanning for repeated queries
Add an Athena partition index on the Glue catalog table — the index allows Athena to look up partition locations in O(log n) rather than O(n), eliminating the full partition metadata scan
Convert the data from Parquet to CSV — CSV files allow Athena to skip rows using WHERE clause pushdown
Increase the Athena query timeout from 30 minutes to 2 hours — timeout causes the vague slow query behavior
explain: For datasets with thousands of partitions, Athena must enumerate all partition metadata from the Glue Data Catalog to determine which partitions satisfy the WHERE clause. Without a partition index, this enumeration is O(n) and adds significant overhead. Athena partition indexes allow binary search on partition key values, reducing metadata lookup from minutes to milliseconds for date-range queries on large partition counts. Parquet already supports predicate pushdown; CSV would make it worse. Result caching only helps repeated identical queries.

q: A data engineer needs to implement cross-account access so that an analytics team in Account B can query a Redshift cluster in Account A using Redshift Data Sharing. Both clusters are ra3.xlplus. What configuration is required?
options: Export the Redshift tables to S3 in Account A and configure S3 cross-account bucket policies for Account B
In Account A, create a datashare and add the Account B Redshift namespace as a consumer. In Account B, create a database from the datashare and grant the analytics team access
Redshift Data Sharing is not supported across AWS accounts — use Redshift Spectrum with a cross-account Glue catalog instead
Create a VPC peering connection between Account A and Account B VPCs — cross-account Redshift access requires network-level connectivity
explain: Redshift Data Sharing allows ra3 clusters to share live data across accounts without data movement or ETL. The producer (Account A) creates a datashare, grants it to Account B's Redshift namespace, and Account B creates a consumer database that reads directly from the producer's storage layer. No VPC peering is required — data sharing uses Redshift's managed network layer. Data Sharing is fully supported cross-account on ra3 and Serverless clusters.

q: A Step Functions state machine orchestrates a 6-step ETL pipeline. Step 3 (a Lambda function) fails intermittently due to transient downstream API timeouts. The entire state machine fails and must be manually restarted from Step 1. How should the engineer fix this?
options: Enable Step Functions Express Workflows — Express Workflows retry failed states automatically without configuration
Add a Wait state of 60 seconds before Step 3 to give the downstream API time to recover
Split the state machine into two separate state machines — Step 1-2 in the first, Steps 4-6 in the second

Add a Retry block to the Task state for Step 3 with ErrorEquals: ['Lambda.ServiceException','States.TaskFailed'], IntervalSeconds: 30, MaxAttempts: 3, BackoffRate: 2 — Step Functions automatically retries Step 3 without restarting the full execution
explain: Step Functions Retry blocks on Task states implement exponential backoff retry logic at the orchestration level without code changes to the Lambda function. The configuration retries Step 3 up to 3 times with a 30-second initial wait, doubling after each attempt. If all retries are exhausted, a Catch block can route to a failure state or DLQ. The state machine execution context is preserved between retries — Steps 1-2 results remain available. Splitting state machines creates coordination overhead. Express Workflows are for high-volume short-duration workflows and do not add automatic retry logic.

q: An EMR Spark job processing daily transaction data runs for 3 hours. CloudWatch metrics show executor CPU is near 100% during the shuffle phase, but 40% of executor memory is unused. The job has 50 executors with 4 cores and 16 GB RAM each. What change MOST reduces runtime?
options: Enable EMR Managed Scaling to automatically add executors when CPU exceeds 80%
Increase executor cores from 4 to 8 per executor — more cores per executor increases parallelism during the CPU-bound shuffle phase
Reduce executor memory to 8 GB — unused memory causes GC overhead that slows shuffle performance
Switch the EMR cluster from on-demand to Spot instances — Spot instances have higher clock speeds
explain: When executor CPU is at 100% and memory is underutilized, the bottleneck is CPU parallelism within each executor. Increasing executor cores from 4 to 8 doubles the number of concurrent Spark tasks per executor, directly addressing the CPU bottleneck during shuffle. The total memory (16 GB) remains adequate per the unused memory observation. EMR Managed Scaling adds new instances but doesn't fix CPU utilization per instance. Reducing memory increases GC pressure. Spot instances have the same CPU specs as on-demand.

q: A data engineer uses AWS Glue Data Quality to validate a daily dataset before loading it to Redshift. The team wants warnings for minor issues but hard stops only for critical failures. How should this be implemented?
options: Use AWS Glue crawlers instead of Data Quality checks — crawlers automatically flag anomalies without stopping the pipeline

Create two separate Glue Data Quality rulesets — one for critical rules and one for advisory rules. Configure the ETL job to fail on critical ruleset failures but log-and-continue on advisory ruleset outcomes using the hasErrors flag
Move data quality checks to Amazon Deequ running on EMR — Deequ supports severity levels natively
Disable Glue Data Quality and implement manual validation in the Lambda function that triggers the Glue job
explain: Glue Data Quality supports multiple rulesets per dataset. By separating critical rules from advisory rules, the ETL job can check the EvaluationResults for each ruleset independently. Critical ruleset failures raise an exception and stop the pipeline. Advisory ruleset results are checked programmatically — if hasErrors is true, the job logs a warning and continues. This pattern implements tiered data quality without external tools.

q: A company uses AWS Lake Formation to control access to a Glue Data Catalog table containing PII data. A new data analyst needs access to all columns except SSN and credit_card_number. What is the CORRECT Lake Formation configuration?
options: Use Lake Formation row filter expressions to hide rows that contain non-null SSN values from the analyst
Create a separate Glue catalog table with the SSN and credit_card_number columns removed and grant the analyst access to that table only
In Lake Formation, grant the analyst SELECT permission on the table with column-level security excluding the SSN and credit_card_number columns
Create an S3 bucket policy that denies the analyst's IAM role access to the specific S3 prefixes containing SSN data
explain: Lake Formation column-level security allows administrators to grant SELECT on specific columns of a table, excluding named columns. When a user queries the table via Athena or Redshift Spectrum, Lake Formation intercepts the query and filters out columns they are not permitted to see. This is enforced at the catalog layer, independent of S3 bucket policies. Row filters apply to row-level security, not column-level restrictions.

q: A Kinesis Data Analytics (Managed Service for Apache Flink) application has processing lag that grows throughout the day. The Flink job has 4 parallelism and checkpointing every 60 seconds. By end of day, checkpoint duration exceeds 5 minutes. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?
options: Managed Flink does not support stateful operations — refactor the fraud detection logic to be stateless
State backend is configured as Memory — state grows throughout the day and large in-memory state causes checkpoint serialization to take progressively longer; switch to RocksDB state backend
The 60-second checkpoint interval is too frequent — reduce to every 10 minutes to allow more processing time
The Kinesis source shard count is too high — reduce shards to match the Flink parallelism setting
explain: Flink checkpointing serializes the entire operator state to durable storage. When the Memory state backend is used, all state is kept in JVM heap. As the application runs throughout the day, state size grows. Large heap state causes checkpoint serialization to take progressively longer. Switching to RocksDB state backend uses incremental checkpointing — only changed state is serialized per checkpoint cycle, keeping checkpoint duration stable regardless of total state size.

q: A data pipeline writes processed records to Amazon DynamoDB using a Glue ETL job on on-demand capacity. During the Glue job, DynamoDB write operations throttle with ProvisionedThroughputExceededException errors. What is the cause?
options: Glue uses a shared write endpoint for DynamoDB with a fixed throughput limit per AWS account
On-demand DynamoDB tables do not support writes from AWS Glue — use provisioned capacity with auto-scaling
The Glue job is writing records larger than 4 KB which DynamoDB counts as multiple write capacity units

DynamoDB on-demand mode scales automatically but has a burst limit — if write traffic increases faster than DynamoDB can scale (more than 2× the previous peak), throttling occurs during the scaling adjustment period
explain: DynamoDB on-demand mode scales automatically but has a warm-up period when traffic suddenly exceeds 2× the table's previous peak. During this ramp-up period, DynamoDB throttles requests while provisioning additional capacity. A Glue ETL job that suddenly generates millions of writes on a table that previously handled low traffic will hit this burst limit. The solution is exponential backoff and retry, pre-warming the table, or spreading writes over time.

q: A regulated company stores customer financial data in Amazon S3. A compliance audit requires proof that all data at rest is encrypted with keys the company controls, and that all key usage is logged for auditing. Which solution satisfies BOTH requirements?
options: Enable S3 Block Public Access and require HTTPS-only bucket policy — these controls satisfy most compliance frameworks
Enable S3 default encryption with SSE-S3 — S3 manages the keys and automatically logs encryption operations to CloudTrail
Use client-side encryption with the AWS Encryption SDK — client-side keys are fully customer-controlled but do not require KMS

Enable S3 default encryption with SSE-KMS using a customer-managed KMS key (CMK). Enable CloudTrail logging for KMS API calls — every S3 object encryption and decryption using the CMK generates a CloudTrail event
explain: SSE-KMS with a customer-managed KMS key satisfies both requirements: (1) the customer creates, owns, and controls the CMK via KMS key policies; (2) every use of the CMK generates a CloudTrail event with the caller's identity, timestamp, and request context, providing a complete audit trail. SSE-S3 uses AWS-managed keys the customer cannot control. Client-side encryption satisfies key control but key usage is not logged to CloudTrail since KMS is not involved.

q: A data engineering team needs to implement column masking so that analysts querying a Redshift table see masked SSN (e.g., '***-**-1234') while data engineers see the full value. Both groups query the same table. Which Redshift feature addresses this?
options: Create two separate Redshift tables — one masked for analysts and one full for engineers. Use scheduled ETL to sync them
Configure a Redshift view with CASE expressions that check current_user() and return masked or full SSN

Use Redshift dynamic data masking (DDM) — define a masking policy that applies a masking expression to the SSN column and attach it to analyst roles while data engineers bypass it with a higher-priority policy
Enable Redshift column-level access control — restrict analysts from reading the SSN column and provide an alternative masked view
explain: Redshift Dynamic Data Masking (DDM) allows administrators to create masking policies and attach them to specific database roles or users. Data engineers with a higher-priority policy see the full value. Analysts with the masking policy see the obfuscated value — all from the same table without duplicates. DDM is transparent to the query. Column-level access control would prevent analysts from reading SSN at all, not mask it.

q: An AWS Glue job processes healthcare data containing PHI. A security review requires sensitive column values never appear in CloudWatch Logs even during debugging. The job currently uses setLogLevel('DEBUG'). What is the CORRECT approach?
options: Set Spark log level to WARN or ERROR to suppress DEBUG-level output that may include data samples, and use Glue continuous logging with custom log filtering to strip PHI patterns before they reach CloudWatch
Store PHI fields in DynamoDB instead of passing them through Glue — DynamoDB does not log field values
Remove all print() and logger statements from the Glue job script and use Glue job metrics instead
Encrypt CloudWatch Logs using a KMS key — encrypted logs protect PHI from unauthorized viewers
explain: Spark's DEBUG log level in Glue can write sample row data to CloudWatch Logs during partition scanning and shuffle phases. Setting log level to WARN or ERROR suppresses data-level log output while preserving error messages needed for operations. Glue continuous logging supports custom filters that can mask or drop log lines matching PHI patterns before delivery to CloudWatch. KMS-encrypted logs still store PHI in plaintext within the encrypted container. Removing all logging makes debugging impossible.

q: An AWS Lake Formation deployment uses tag-based access control (TBAC). A data steward needs to grant a new data scientist access to all tables tagged sensitivity=low across 5 different Glue databases. What is the MOST EFFICIENT approach?
options: Create a Glue crawler that moves all sensitivity=low tables to a dedicated database and grant database-level access
Use IAM resource-based policies on each Glue database to allow access to tables with the sensitivity=low tag
Grant SELECT permission individually on each sensitivity=low table in each of the 5 databases

Create a Lake Formation LF-tag permission: grant SELECT on LF-tag key=sensitivity, value=low for the data scientist's IAM role — any table tagged sensitivity=low across all databases is automatically included
explain: Lake Formation Tag-Based Access Control (TBAC) allows a single permission grant on an LF-tag expression to automatically cover all catalog resources that carry that tag — regardless of which database they reside in. As new sensitivity=low tables are added and tagged, the data scientist automatically gains access without additional grants. Individual table grants require O(n) operations and ongoing maintenance. IAM resource tags cannot enforce Glue catalog access — Lake Formation tag permissions are enforced at the catalog layer.

q: A Glue PySpark job reads compressed JSON files from S3 (averaging 200 MB each, gzip-compressed) and experiences poor parallelism — only 2 Glue workers are active despite 20 allocated. What is the ROOT CAUSE?
options: JSON format does not support Spark parallel reads — use CSV or Parquet for multi-worker parallelism
The Glue job is configured with G.1X workers which have only 1 vCPU each and cannot parallelize reading
Gzip-compressed files are not splittable — each file must be decompressed and read by a single Spark task, so parallelism is limited to the number of files
S3 has a per-prefix rate limit that limits parallel file reads to 2 workers
explain: Gzip is not a splittable compression format — Spark cannot read a gzip file from an arbitrary byte offset without decompressing from the beginning. This means each gzip file is assigned to exactly one Spark task. With only 2 gzip files available at a time, only 2 workers are active regardless of the total worker count. The fix is to re-store files in a splittable format (Parquet with snappy or ORC) so Spark can split large files across multiple tasks.

q: A data engineer needs to migrate 50 TB of historical data from an on-premises Oracle database to Amazon S3 in Parquet format. The migration must complete within 48 hours and the on-premises network bandwidth is 1 Gbps. What migration approach is MOST LIKELY to meet the deadline?
options: Export Oracle data to CSV and upload to S3 using the AWS CLI sync command with 100 parallel threads
Use AWS Direct Connect with a 10 Gbps dedicated line — a 10 Gbps connection transfers 50 TB in approximately 11 hours
Use AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) full-load task — DMS streams data over the network and converts to Parquet format automatically

Use AWS Snowball Edge Compute Optimized devices — load the Oracle data export files onto Snowball Edge on-premises, ship them to AWS, and AWS ingests to S3. At 1 Gbps, transferring 50 TB over the network alone would take ~111 hours, far exceeding 48 hours
explain: At 1 Gbps network bandwidth, transferring 50 TB takes approximately 111 hours — far exceeding the 48-hour window. AWS Snowball Edge devices are shipped to the customer, loaded with data on-premises, and physically transported to AWS for ingestion. Physical transport bypasses the network bottleneck entirely. A 10 Gbps Direct Connect would work mathematically but requires procurement and installation that cannot be completed in 48 hours. DMS streams over the existing 1 Gbps connection and faces the same 111-hour constraint.

q: A company streams financial transactions into Amazon Kinesis Data Streams with 10 shards. A downstream Flink application on Amazon Managed Streaming for Apache Flink must detect fraudulent patterns within a 5-minute tumbling window. The Flink job fails to maintain low latency because state checkpoints take too long. What is the MOST EFFECTIVE solution?
options: Increase Kinesis shard count to 50 — more shards reduce per-shard volume and checkpoint size automatically
Switch from MSF to Kinesis Data Analytics SQL — SQL applications have built-in checkpoint management
Reduce the tumbling window size from 5 minutes to 30 seconds — smaller windows reduce the amount of state that must be checkpointed

Enable Flink incremental checkpointing with RocksDB state backend — only state changes (deltas) are written during each checkpoint instead of the full state snapshot, dramatically reducing checkpoint duration
explain: Flink incremental checkpointing (available only with the RocksDB state backend) writes only the state changes since the last successful checkpoint, rather than the entire state snapshot. For stateful fraud detection with 5-minute windows, total accumulated state can be large, but the delta per checkpoint interval is much smaller. This keeps checkpoint duration consistently low. Increasing Kinesis shards changes the parallelism but not the checkpoint mechanism. Reducing window size sacrifices detection accuracy.

q: A data engineer builds an AWS Glue job that reads customer order data from RDS MySQL (5 million rows) and performs complex multi-table joins before writing to S3. The Glue job fails with an OutOfMemoryError during the join phase. The job uses G.1X workers with 4 workers total. What is the BEST fix?
options: Switch the Glue job type from Spark to Python Shell — Python Shell jobs use a single large-memory node suitable for in-memory joins
Upgrade to G.2X workers and increase worker count to 10 — G.2X workers have 16 GB RAM each and more workers distribute the join data across more executors
Enable Glue job bookmarks — bookmarks prevent reprocessing already-joined data, reducing the total dataset size
Use Glue pushdown predicates to filter rows before the join — if the join inputs are smaller, the join requires less memory
explain: Glue G.1X workers provide 4 vCPUs and 8 GB RAM. A 5-million-row multi-table join that exceeds memory limits requires either more memory per worker (G.2X = 8 vCPUs, 16 GB RAM) or more workers to distribute the shuffle and join partitions. Upgrading to G.2X doubles available RAM while adding workers increases the number of partitions the join is distributed across. Python Shell is a single-node non-Spark environment entirely unsuitable for distributed SQL joins on millions of rows.

q: A data team stores clickstream events in S3 as JSON files partitioned by date. Athena queries filtering by user_id take 4-5 minutes and scan 2 TB of data even when filtering for a single user. The date partition alone does not reduce scan volume sufficiently. What additional optimization MOST reduces per-query scan cost?
options: Convert JSON to Parquet with columnar compression — Parquet reduces per-column scan but cannot filter rows by user_id without reading the full file
Create a secondary index on user_id in the Glue Data Catalog — Glue supports secondary indexes that Athena uses to skip non-matching rows
Enable Athena federated query to query DynamoDB instead — DynamoDB supports key-based lookups that scan only the target user's records

Implement Z-order clustering or sort the Parquet files by user_id within each date partition — combined with Parquet row group statistics, Athena can skip entire row groups whose user_id range does not match the filter
explain: Within a Parquet file, data is organized into row groups. Each row group has min/max statistics per column. If Parquet files are sorted or Z-ordered by user_id, all rows for a given user_id fall within a small number of row groups. When Athena applies a user_id filter, it reads the row group statistics and skips row groups whose range does not contain the target value — reducing actual bytes scanned from 2 TB to megabytes for single-user queries. Glue Data Catalog does not support row-level secondary indexes.

q: A company uses Amazon S3 as a data lake and Apache Iceberg table format for ACID transactions. A data engineer runs a DELETE statement for a specific customer's GDPR erasure request. The next day, the deleted records are still accessible via time-travel queries using older snapshot IDs. What must be done to fully satisfy the erasure request?
options: Run Iceberg's expireSnapshots procedure to remove old snapshot references, then delete the orphaned S3 data files that previously contained the customer records
The time-travel data is stored in S3 Glacier which is read-only — contact AWS Support to purge the Glacier objects
Archive the Iceberg table to a new S3 bucket — data in the new bucket has no historical snapshots
Re-run the DELETE statement — the first DELETE may have failed silently
explain: Apache Iceberg implements ACID deletes by writing new data files and updating metadata — the old data files become orphaned. Time-travel queries use old snapshot metadata to read these orphaned files. To physically erase the data: (1) run expireSnapshots to remove snapshot metadata references older than a specified timestamp; (2) delete the orphaned S3 data files using Iceberg's orphan file removal procedure. Only after the underlying S3 objects are deleted is the data truly erased. This is the correct GDPR right-to-erasure process for Iceberg tables.

q: A data engineer needs to implement a slowly changing dimension (SCD Type 2) in Amazon Redshift. When a customer's address changes, the old record must be preserved with an end_date and a new record inserted with is_current=true. Which approach BEST supports this pattern?
options: Use a staging table with an explicit two-step process in a single transaction: (1) UPDATE the existing current record to set end_date and is_current=false; (2) INSERT the new current record from the staging table
Enable Redshift Auto Vacuum — Auto Vacuum automatically handles SCD Type 2 dimension updates
Redshift does not support UPDATE operations — use DELETE and re-INSERT for all dimension changes
Use Redshift MERGE (UPSERT) command — MERGE automatically handles SCD Type 2 by updating end_date and inserting the new record in a single statement
explain: SCD Type 2 requires two operations: closing the existing current record (UPDATE) and inserting the new version (INSERT). In Redshift, the correct pattern uses a staging table and wraps both operations in a single transaction for atomicity. The UPDATE marks old records as expired. The INSERT adds the new current records. Redshift's MERGE command supports UPSERT (UPDATE or INSERT) but not SCD Type 2's pattern of closing and inserting simultaneously — it updates in-place. Redshift fully supports UPDATE operations.

q: A company uses AWS Data Exchange to publish a curated financial dataset. After a major data quality fix, the data engineer needs to revise the published dataset and ensure all subscribers automatically receive the updated version without re-subscribing. Which Data Exchange feature enables this?
options: Use CloudFront in front of the Data Exchange S3 bucket — CloudFront invalidations propagate updates to subscribers transparently
Create an SNS notification to alert subscribers to manually re-download the updated dataset
Delete and republish the dataset with a new product listing — subscribers must manually subscribe to the new listing
Use AWS Data Exchange for S3 to publish revised data — when the provider publishes a new revision, all active subscribers automatically receive the updated data files
explain: AWS Data Exchange for S3 organizes published datasets into revisions. When a provider publishes a new revision to an existing product, all subscribers with active subscriptions automatically receive the updated data according to their configured export settings. This is the designed workflow for dataset updates — providers iterate on revisions without requiring subscribers to take any action. Creating a new product listing breaks existing subscriptions.

q: A Glue ETL job that loads data into Redshift fails when the Redshift cluster is in maintenance mode during the Glue job window. This has happened three times in the past month. How should the data engineer make the pipeline resilient?
options: Switch from Glue to an EMR cluster for the ETL job — EMR is not affected by Redshift maintenance windows

Use an AWS Step Functions state machine to orchestrate the Glue job. Add an error Catch for connection failures with a retry using exponential backoff and an SNS notification after max retries are exhausted
Disable Redshift automated maintenance windows — turn off maintenance to prevent conflicts with ETL schedules
Add a 10-minute sleep at the start of the Glue job script to avoid the first minutes of maintenance windows
explain: Step Functions provides native retry and error handling at the orchestration level. Wrapping the Glue job in a Task state with Catch and Retry blocks allows the pipeline to automatically retry when Redshift is temporarily unavailable during maintenance (typically 30 minutes). The exponential backoff gives Redshift time to complete maintenance before the next retry. SNS notification after max retries alerts the team for manual intervention. Disabling maintenance windows violates AWS operational best practices and prevents critical security patches.

q: A data platform team uses Apache Airflow on Amazon MWAA to orchestrate 200+ DAGs. When a critical upstream DAG fails, 15 dependent downstream DAGs continue to run on stale data and succeed — producing incorrect results. How should the team prevent downstream DAGs from running when an upstream DAG fails?
options: Delete failed DAG runs from the Airflow metadata database — Airflow automatically re-evaluates dependencies after database cleanup
Use Airflow ExternalTaskSensor in each downstream DAG to wait for the upstream DAG's task to reach 'success' state before proceeding — if the upstream fails, the sensor blocks downstream execution
Set the schedule_interval to None on all downstream DAGs — manual triggers prevent them from running automatically
Enable MWAA environment auto-scaling — more workers process the upstream DAG faster, reducing failure probability
explain: Airflow's ExternalTaskSensor polls an external DAG's task state on a configurable interval. Downstream DAGs configured with ExternalTaskSensor wait in a running state until the upstream task reaches success. If the upstream task fails, the sensor times out — preventing the downstream DAG from proceeding with stale data. Setting schedule_interval=None breaks automation entirely. MWAA auto-scaling affects worker availability, not DAG dependency logic.

q: An AWS Glue job processes 10 million records daily and uses the DynamicFrame relationalize() method to flatten deeply nested JSON structures. The job runs for 6 hours. What is the BEST alternative approach to reduce runtime?
options: Replace Glue DynamicFrames entirely with pandas DataFrames — pandas processes JSON faster than Spark for datasets under 100 million rows

Convert the DynamicFrames to Spark DataFrames and use Spark SQL's built-in functions (explode, struct, get_json_object) to flatten the nested structure — native Spark SQL functions execute in the JVM without Python serialization overhead
Replace relationalize() with a custom PySpark UDF using Python's json.loads() — UDFs are faster than DynamicFrame transformations
Use AWS Lambda to pre-flatten the JSON before Glue reads it — JSON flattening in Lambda is faster than Spark DynamicFrame transformations
explain: Glue's DynamicFrame.relationalize() is a convenience method but is not optimized for high-throughput production workloads — it creates intermediate temporary S3 directories and is slow for deeply nested structures. Converting to a Spark DataFrame and using native Spark SQL functions (explode() for arrays, inline() for struct arrays) leverages Spark's Catalyst optimizer and executes JVM-native code without Python serialization. PySpark UDFs with Python json.loads() are slower than native Spark SQL functions because each row crosses the Python-JVM boundary.

q: A company stores highly sensitive research data in S3. A security audit recommends adding a bucket policy requiring MFA for delete operations. Which bucket policy condition key enforces MFA for s3:DeleteObject?
options: aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent: true combined with a Deny if false — denies s3:DeleteObject requests where the requester's session was not authenticated with MFA
aws:PrincipalType: MFAUser — this condition key matches only IAM users who have completed MFA authentication
s3:MFADelete: enabled — enabling MFA Delete on the bucket automatically requires MFA for all delete API calls
aws:RequestedRegion — ensures delete operations can only be performed from specific AWS regions where MFA is enforced
explain: The IAM condition key aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent evaluates to true when the API call is made using credentials from an MFA-authenticated session. A bucket policy Deny statement with Condition: Bool: aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent: false blocks any s3:DeleteObject call made without MFA authentication. Note: MFA Delete (s3:MFADelete) is a separate Versioning feature requiring the physical MFA device serial number in the API call header — it is not a condition key. aws:RequestedRegion controls region, not authentication method.

q: A streaming pipeline ingests stock trade events via Kinesis Data Streams and uses a Lambda function to write enriched records to Amazon OpenSearch Service. During market hours, trade volume spikes 10x and OpenSearch write latency increases, causing Lambda timeout errors. What architectural change MOST reduces Lambda timeouts?
options: Increase Kinesis shard count from 5 to 50 — more shards spread Lambda invocations across more concurrent functions

Add Kinesis Data Firehose between Kinesis Data Streams and OpenSearch — Firehose buffers records, batches them, and handles OpenSearch write backpressure with built-in retry, decoupling Lambda from OpenSearch latency spikes
Set Lambda reserved concurrency to 1 — limiting concurrency prevents Lambda from overwhelming OpenSearch
Use SQS as a buffer between Lambda and OpenSearch — Lambda writes to SQS and a separate consumer reads from SQS and writes to OpenSearch at a controlled rate
explain: Kinesis Data Firehose natively integrates with Amazon OpenSearch Service and handles all buffering, batching, retry, and backpressure logic automatically. During OpenSearch latency spikes, Firehose buffers incoming records from Kinesis and retries failed OpenSearch writes with exponential backoff — without any Lambda involvement. The Lambda function would only write to Kinesis for enrichment, and Firehose handles the rest. This completely decouples Lambda from OpenSearch write latency.

q: A data lake on Amazon S3 uses Apache Hudi table format for upserts. A data engineer runs hourly compaction jobs to merge delta logs into base files. The compaction job now takes longer than 1 hour, causing it to overlap with the next scheduled run. What is the MOST APPROPRIATE fix?
options: Switch from Hudi to Apache Iceberg — Iceberg compaction is always faster than Hudi compaction
Increase the Hudi write batch size — larger batches produce fewer delta log files, reducing the amount that compaction must merge
Run compaction less frequently — compact once per day instead of hourly to reduce job frequency
Enable Hudi async (non-blocking) compaction — compaction runs in the background without blocking ingestion writes, preventing scheduling overlap
explain: Hudi asynchronous compaction decouples the compaction process from the ingestion write path. Ingestion continues writing new delta logs while compaction runs independently on a separate cluster or thread. This prevents the compaction job from blocking or overlapping ingestion scheduling. Running compaction less frequently increases the delta log backlog, making each compaction job larger and slower over time.

q: A company needs to build a near-real-time reporting dashboard showing sales totals updated every 5 minutes. The source data is in Amazon RDS PostgreSQL and the pipeline must handle both inserts and updates to existing sales records. Which approach is MOST APPROPRIATE?
options: Run a full export of the PostgreSQL table every 5 minutes using pg_dump and load it into Redshift — full loads ensure all updates are captured

Use AWS DMS with CDC to stream inserts and updates from RDS PostgreSQL to Kinesis Data Streams, then use Kinesis Firehose to deliver changes to S3, and a Glue job every 5 minutes to merge the changes into Redshift
Configure PostgreSQL logical replication to a Kinesis Data Stream and use Lambda to apply inserts and updates directly to Redshift using MERGE statements
Use an RDS read replica and connect Redshift Spectrum directly to the read replica — Spectrum queries the live replica every 5 minutes
explain: AWS DMS CDC captures row-level changes from the PostgreSQL WAL and streams them to Kinesis in near-real-time. Kinesis Firehose buffers and delivers to S3 with configurable latency. A Glue job running every 5 minutes reads new CDC files from S3 and applies MERGE operations to Redshift — handling both inserts and updates correctly. Full pg_dump every 5 minutes is massively resource-intensive. Redshift Spectrum cannot query RDS directly. Direct Lambda-to-Redshift MERGE has connection pooling challenges at scale.

q: A data engineer uses AWS Glue job bookmarks to enable incremental processing of new S3 files. After a schema change in the source data, the Glue job needs to reprocess all historical files from the beginning. What is the CORRECT procedure?
options: Create a new Glue job with a different name — bookmarks are stored per job name, and a fresh job has no bookmark history
Update the Glue job script to ignore the bookmark state by deleting the JobBookmarkEntry from the job parameters
Reset the Glue job bookmark using the AWS console (Job bookmark → Reset) or the AWS CLI resetJobBookmark API — this clears the bookmark state so the next run processes all files from the beginning
Delete all S3 source files and reload them — Glue bookmarks track files by S3 key, and re-uploading files makes them appear new
explain: Glue job bookmarks track which S3 files or JDBC offsets have been successfully processed. To reprocess all historical data, the bookmark state must be reset — done via the Glue console or the CLI command aws glue reset-job-bookmark. After reset, the next job run processes all input files from the beginning as if it were the first run. Creating a new job also works but loses all job run history. Deleting and re-uploading S3 files is operationally risky for large datasets.

q: A financial services company uses Amazon Redshift for analytics. Auditors require that all queries run against the Redshift cluster are logged with user identity, query text, and timestamp, and that these logs cannot be deleted or modified for 3 years. Which configuration satisfies BOTH the logging and immutability requirements?
options: Use Amazon RDS instead of Redshift — RDS has built-in query log immutability for compliance workloads
Enable CloudTrail logging for the Redshift account — CloudTrail logs all API calls including query execution
Enable Redshift audit logging to an S3 bucket. Enable S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode with a 3-year retention period on the audit log bucket
Enable Redshift Enhanced VPC Routing and configure VPC Flow Logs — flow logs capture network-level query activity
explain: Redshift audit logging writes all query text and user identity to S3. Enabling S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode with a 3-year retention period makes these log files immutable — even the root account cannot delete or modify them before the retention period expires. CloudTrail logs Redshift API calls but does not capture individual SQL query text run within the cluster. VPC Flow Logs capture IP-level network traffic, not SQL query content.

q: A streaming pipeline needs to calculate the running total purchase amount per customer and emit an alert when any customer exceeds $10,000 in a single day. Which AWS service and feature BEST handles this stateful aggregation with daily resets?
options: AWS Glue Streaming job with a sliding window of 24 hours per customer — Glue Streaming computes running totals across sliding windows

Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics (Managed Flink) with a tumbling window of 86,400 seconds grouped by customer_id — Flink maintains per-customer running totals within the window and resets at the window boundary
Amazon SQS FIFO queue with a Lambda consumer — FIFO ordering guarantees per-customer events are processed in order, enabling accurate running totals
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams with a Lambda consumer that writes running totals to DynamoDB — Lambda reads per-customer totals, adds the new purchase, writes back, and checks the threshold
explain: Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink natively supports stateful stream processing with time-based windows. A tumbling window of 86,400 seconds partitioned by customer_id accumulates purchase amounts per customer within the window, resets at the daily boundary, and can trigger alerts when aggregated totals exceed thresholds. Flink manages the per-customer state durably with checkpointing. The Lambda + DynamoDB pattern has race conditions under concurrent updates for the same customer_id (requires conditional writes). Glue Streaming does not support tumbling windows with threshold alerts.

q: A data team deploys Amazon DataZone as a data marketplace. Business analysts in Domain A need to discover and subscribe to datasets owned by Domain B. The Domain B data steward must approve all subscription requests. How is this workflow implemented in DataZone?
options: Domain B publishes datasets to a public S3 bucket — analysts from Domain A access the S3 bucket directly after signing an internal data sharing agreement
Use Lake Formation cross-database grants — Lake Formation sends email notifications to data owners when new grants are requested

In DataZone, Domain B publishes data assets to the catalog with a subscription grant requiring data steward approval. Domain A analysts submit subscription requests through the DataZone portal. Domain B's data steward approves/denies the request — upon approval, DataZone automatically provisions access
Create IAM cross-account roles manually for each analyst who needs access — IAM role assumption is the approval mechanism in DataZone
explain: Amazon DataZone implements a governed data marketplace workflow: data producers publish assets to the catalog; consumers submit subscription requests; the data steward receives a notification and approves/rejects; upon approval, DataZone automatically provisions the necessary access (Lake Formation grants, Redshift permissions, or S3 access) without manual IAM configuration. This end-to-end workflow is DataZone's core value proposition over ad-hoc sharing mechanisms.

q: A company's data engineering team accidentally pushed AWS access keys to a public GitHub repository. The keys were live for 4 hours before being detected and have now been deactivated. What additional steps are REQUIRED to fully remediate the incident?
options: Delete the IAM user associated with the leaked keys and create a new user — deleting the user invalidates all associated keys
Change the GitHub repository from public to private — this removes the keys from public view
Deactivating the keys is sufficient — deactivated keys cannot be used for any AWS API calls

Review CloudTrail logs for the 4-hour exposure window to identify any unauthorized API calls. Remediate any resources created or modified during that period. Rotate all other credentials if accessed via the leaked keys. Enable GuardDuty to detect future credential misuse
explain: Key deactivation stops future misuse but does not reveal what happened during the exposure window. A complete incident response requires: (1) CloudTrail analysis to identify all API calls made with the leaked credentials during the 4-hour window — including new resource creation, data exfiltration, or permission escalation; (2) remediation of any unauthorized changes; (3) rotation of any downstream secrets; (4) enabling GuardDuty for ongoing anomaly detection. Making the GitHub repo private only prevents future discovery — the keys were already indexed by scanners within seconds of publication.

q: A company runs nightly AWS Glue ETL jobs that read from S3 and write results to Redshift. On some nights, the source S3 data is late (arrives 2-4 hours past the scheduled start). Currently the Glue jobs fail with 'No files found' errors. What is the BEST architectural fix?
options: Add a retry loop inside the Glue job script that checks S3 every 10 minutes for up to 4 hours before failing
Schedule Glue jobs 4 hours later to accommodate the latest possible data arrival time
Use Amazon EventBridge with an S3 event notification rule — trigger the Glue job only when a specific '_SUCCESS' marker file is written to the S3 prefix, indicating data is ready
Use AWS Step Functions with a Wait state of 4 hours before calling the Glue job — the fixed wait period absorbs the worst-case delay
explain: Event-driven triggering eliminates the problem entirely by decoupling job execution from a fixed schedule. The upstream pipeline writes a manifest/success file as its final step, which triggers the downstream Glue job immediately when data is ready — whether on time, 2 hours late, or 4 hours late. A fixed schedule still fails if data is later than expected. Retry loops waste cluster time and cost money during waiting. A fixed 4-hour Step Functions Wait adds unnecessary delay when data arrives early.

q: An Amazon Athena query against a large partitioned S3 table returns different results each time it runs on the same data. The underlying S3 data has not changed. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?
options: The Glue Data Catalog partition metadata is stale — new partitions exist in S3 that have not been added to the catalog, causing Athena to include or exclude partitions non-deterministically
Athena query result caching is enabled and returning stale cached results from earlier queries that ran against different data
Athena does not support deterministic queries on Parquet files larger than 1 GB
Athena uses eventual consistency when reading from S3 — concurrent writes by other processes create read inconsistency
explain: Athena query result reuse stores query results for up to 7 days. If a cached result exists for an identical query string, Athena returns the cached result without re-executing the query. If the underlying data was different when the cached result was created (e.g., data was reprocessed), the cached result will not reflect the current data — causing apparent non-determinism. The fix is to disable result caching for queries where data freshness is critical. S3 provides strong read-after-write consistency for all operations since December 2020.

q: A data engineer needs to set up an automated data pipeline that reads from a cross-account S3 bucket (Account A) and writes processed results to DynamoDB in Account B. The Glue job runs in Account B. What IAM configuration is required?
options: In Account A, create an S3 bucket policy that grants s3:GetObject to Account B's Glue IAM role ARN. In Account B, attach an IAM role to the Glue job with s3:GetObject on Account A's bucket ARN and dynamodb:PutItem on Account B's table
Use VPC peering between Account A and Account B — cross-account S3 access requires network-level connectivity
Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on Account A's bucket — Transfer Acceleration supports cross-account read access automatically
