Create an IAM user in Account A with S3 read permissions and embed the access key in the Glue job script environment variables
explain: Cross-account S3 access requires a two-way trust: (1) the S3 bucket in Account A must have a resource-based bucket policy explicitly granting the Account B IAM role permission to read objects; (2) the Account B IAM role must have an identity-based policy allowing s3:GetObject on Account A's bucket ARN. Both conditions must be true simultaneously. IAM users with embedded access keys are a security anti-pattern. VPC peering is not required for S3 access which uses HTTPS endpoints.

q: A company discovers that an AWS Glue job has been writing processed data to an S3 bucket without encrypting data in transit. The security team requires all S3 API calls from Glue to use HTTPS. What is the CORRECT enforcement mechanism?
options: Configure the Glue job's network settings to use a VPC with a NAT Gateway — NAT Gateway enforces TLS for all outbound traffic
Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration — accelerated transfers automatically use TLS encryption for all data in transit
Add a bucket policy with a Deny statement for aws:SecureTransport: false — this denies any request to the bucket that does not use HTTPS, regardless of the requester
Enable S3 server-side encryption (SSE-S3) — server-side encryption protects data in transit as well as at rest
explain: An S3 bucket policy with the condition aws:SecureTransport: false combined with a Deny effect blocks all requests that do not use HTTPS. This is a standard AWS security control that applies to all requesters — Glue jobs, CLI users, SDK calls — regardless of IAM permissions. SSE-S3 encrypts data at rest inside S3 storage but does not protect data in transit between the Glue worker and S3. VPC/NAT settings do not enforce TLS application-layer encryption.

q: A streaming pipeline ingests sensor data from 50,000 IoT devices using Kinesis Data Streams with 20 shards. A Lambda consumer processes events and stores aggregations in DynamoDB. Some devices' events are processed out of order. What is the ROOT CAUSE?
options: Device events from the same device are written to different shards because the partition key is set to a random UUID instead of device_id — Kinesis only guarantees ordering within a shard
Lambda has a concurrency limit of 1,000 and concurrent invocations process shard records out of sequence
Lambda processes Kinesis records in random order regardless of sequence number — Lambda's event-driven architecture does not preserve Kinesis shard ordering
Kinesis Data Streams delivers records at-least-once by default, causing duplicates that appear as out-of-order events
explain: Kinesis guarantees ordering only within a single shard. If a partition key maps events from the same device to multiple shards (random UUID partition key), those events are processed in parallel by different Lambda invocations with no ordering guarantee between shards. The fix is to use device_id as the Kinesis partition key — this ensures all events from a given device always go to the same shard and are processed in sequence number order within that shard.

q: A Glue ETL job joins a large S3 dataset with a DynamoDB lookup table containing 2 million enrichment records. The job runs for 4 hours because every record triggers an individual DynamoDB GetItem call. What optimization MOST reduces runtime?
options: Use DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) in front of DynamoDB — DAX caches GetItem responses and serves cached results for repeated lookups
Use DynamoDB BatchGetItem inside the Glue job — replace individual GetItem calls with BatchGetItem to fetch up to 100 records per API call
Enable DynamoDB Streams on the lookup table — streams pre-fetch the records Glue needs before the job starts
Export the DynamoDB lookup table to S3 using DynamoDB Export, then load it as a broadcast table in the Glue job — eliminate all runtime DynamoDB API calls by joining against the S3 snapshot
explain: Individual DynamoDB GetItem calls from Glue incur network round-trip latency for every record. With millions of records, this serializes into hours of I/O wait. The optimal solution is to pre-export the 2-million-row lookup table to S3 and load it as a Spark broadcast table in the Glue job. The small lookup table (~1 GB) fits comfortably in Spark executor memory and eliminates all DynamoDB API calls during processing. BatchGetItem reduces API calls 100x but still involves 20,000 network requests with batching overhead.

q: A data governance requirement states that Athena query results must never be stored outside the company's specific S3 bucket (results-bucket) even if a user tries to specify a different output location. How can this be enforced?
options: Configure Athena query result encryption using a KMS key that only grants access to the results-bucket

Create an Athena workgroup with a fixed query result location set to the approved S3 bucket and enable the 'Override client-side settings' option — this forces all queries in the workgroup to write results to the configured bucket regardless of user-specified locations
Use S3 bucket policies on all other S3 buckets to deny the Athena service principal
Configure IAM policies on each analyst's role to deny s3:PutObject on all buckets except results-bucket
explain: Athena workgroups provide centralized control over query execution settings. When a workgroup is configured with a specific S3 output location and the 'Override client-side settings' flag is enabled, all queries in that workgroup write results to the configured S3 location — the user's specified output location is ignored. Combined with IAM policies restricting which workgroup a user can use, this enforces output location compliance without complex bucket-level policies across all S3 buckets.

q: A data quality team needs to attach business-level metadata (owner, quality score, data classification) to Glue catalog tables without modifying existing table schemas. Which Glue feature supports this?
options: Use AWS Resource Groups to tag Glue catalog tables — Resource Group tags appear in the Glue console alongside table definitions
Use Glue table tags — key-value pairs attached to catalog tables store business metadata searchable via the console and API
Create separate Glue catalog tables with suffix '_metadata' to store business metadata alongside technical table definitions

Use Glue catalog table parameters (table properties) to store custom key-value metadata — properties appear in the catalog alongside schema information and are queryable via the API and console without schema changes
explain: AWS Glue Data Catalog tables support custom table properties — arbitrary key-value pairs stored in the catalog's table definition metadata. These properties can store owner names, quality scores, data classification labels, and any other business metadata without modifying the underlying table schema. They are retrievable via the GetTable API and visible in the Glue console. Resource Group tags apply to AWS resources but are not embedded in individual table definitions visible alongside column definitions.

q: A Redshift cluster runs complex analytical queries that frequently spill to disk, increasing query times 3-5x. CloudWatch shows available memory at 85% utilization. The cluster is ra3.4xlarge with 4 nodes and the data engineer cannot increase node count due to budget constraints. What configuration change MOST reduces disk spill?
options: Decrease the WLM concurrency level from 5 to 2 — fewer concurrent queries means each query receives a larger memory allocation per slot, reducing the likelihood of spilling to disk
Enable Redshift automatic table optimization (ATO) — ATO restructures table distribution and sort keys to reduce data each query must process
Enable Redshift Enhanced VPC Routing — VPC routing reduces query latency, indirectly reducing memory pressure
Increase the WLM concurrency level from 5 to 15 — more concurrent slots allow queries to share memory more efficiently
explain: Redshift allocates query memory by dividing the total available memory by the WLM concurrency level. At concurrency=5 with 85% memory utilization, each concurrent query receives relatively little memory. Complex analytical queries that require large hash tables or sort buffers exceed their slot's memory allocation and spill to disk. Reducing concurrency to 2 doubles each query's memory allocation, allowing more of the query's working set to remain in memory. The trade-off is reduced throughput, which is acceptable if the primary workload is a small number of complex analytical queries.

q: A data engineer needs to schedule an AWS Glue job to run exactly once when a specific S3 prefix receives its daily data file, indicated by a _SUCCESS marker file. The file arrives at variable times between midnight and 6 AM. Which trigger configuration achieves event-driven execution?
options: Use a Glue event-based trigger with an EventBridge rule that fires on S3 ObjectCreated events matching the _SUCCESS key suffix — when the marker file is written, EventBridge triggers the Glue job within minutes
Set a Glue scheduled trigger for 6 AM daily — the latest possible arrival time ensures data is always present
Configure an S3 Event Notification directly to Glue — S3 natively supports Glue as a target for object creation events
Use a Glue conditional trigger that checks whether the S3 file exists every 5 minutes using a Lambda polling function
explain: AWS Glue event-based triggers integrate with Amazon EventBridge. An EventBridge rule with a pattern matching S3 ObjectCreated events for the specific prefix and key suffix (_SUCCESS) fires within minutes of the marker file being written to S3. The Glue event trigger listens for this EventBridge event and starts the job. A 6 AM scheduled trigger runs even when data has not arrived (causing failure) or adds unnecessary delay when data arrives at 12:30 AM. S3 Event Notifications can target Lambda, SQS, or SNS but not Glue directly.

q: A healthcare company stores patient records in a Redshift cluster. HIPAA requires that PHI column values are masked for analysts but visible to medical staff using the same table. The company also needs an audit trail of who accessed which PHI records and when. Which combination of Redshift features satisfies BOTH requirements?
options: Use Redshift Row-Level Security (RLS) to hide PHI rows from analysts — RLS prevents analysts from seeing any PHI records, and user activity logging captures all query text
Create two Redshift clusters — one for analysts (PHI masked at ingestion) and one for medical staff (full data). Use CloudTrail to log cluster-level API calls

Use Redshift Dynamic Data Masking (DDM) to mask PHI columns for analyst roles while medical staff roles bypass masking. Enable Redshift audit logging (user activity log) to capture all query text with user identity and timestamp to S3
Use Redshift column-level access control to deny analysts SELECT on PHI columns and rely on Redshift connection logs for auditing
explain: Redshift DDM masks column values based on the querying user's role — analysts see masked PHI while medical staff see actual values. Both groups query the same table with no data duplication. Redshift user activity logging (enabled via parameter group: enable_user_activity_logging=true) writes every SQL statement to S3, including the user identity, database name, timestamp, and full query text — creating a complete PHI access audit trail. Column-level access control prevents analysts from reading PHI entirely, breaking analyst workflows. RLS hides entire rows, not specific columns.

q: A Glue job reads data from S3 and writes to a Redshift cluster using the Glue JDBC connector. The job fails with a connection timeout during the write phase. The Glue job runs in a VPC and the Redshift cluster is in the same VPC. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?
options: The Redshift cluster's security group does not have an inbound rule allowing traffic on port 5439 from the Glue job's elastic network interface (ENI) security group
The Glue job IAM role lacks redshift:GetClusterCredentials permission — required for JDBC connections
The Glue VPC subnet must be in the same Availability Zone as the Redshift cluster's leader node for JDBC connections to succeed
The Glue JDBC connector does not support VPC-based Redshift clusters — use the native Glue for Redshift connector instead
explain: When a Glue job runs in a VPC, it creates Elastic Network Interfaces (ENIs) in the specified VPC subnet. All traffic from the Glue workers originates from these ENIs, which are associated with the Glue job's security group. For the JDBC connection to Redshift (port 5439) to succeed, the Redshift cluster's security group must have an inbound rule allowing port 5439 from the Glue job's security group ID. Without this inbound rule, connection attempts time out. IAM permissions control Redshift API calls, not JDBC network connectivity.

q: A company uses AWS Transfer Family (SFTP) to receive large CSV files from external partners daily. A Glue job then processes these files. The Glue job fails when a partner uploads an incomplete file (transfer interrupted mid-upload). How should the data engineer prevent Glue from processing incomplete files?
options: Enable S3 Versioning on the bucket — versioned uploads create a complete object only after all parts are uploaded

Require partners to upload a separate SHA256 checksum file after uploading the data file. Configure an S3 Event Notification on the checksum file's suffix to trigger the Glue job — Glue only starts when the partner explicitly signals data is complete
Configure Glue job bookmarks — bookmarks skip files that were not completely written
Enable AWS Transfer Family managed workflows — add a step to validate file completeness before moving the file to the Glue input prefix
explain: AWS Transfer Family managed workflows define a sequence of steps that execute when a file upload completes on the SFTP server. A workflow step can call a Lambda function to validate file integrity (check file size, validate CSV row count, or verify checksum), and only move the validated file to the Glue input S3 prefix upon successful validation. Incomplete or corrupt files are moved to an error prefix and the Glue job is never triggered. Job bookmarks track processed files, not file completeness. S3 Versioning creates valid (but truncated) S3 objects for interrupted uploads.

q: A company stores audit logs in S3 in a dedicated logging account. The security policy requires that log files cannot be deleted or overwritten for 90 days after creation, and this restriction applies even if the logging account root user credentials are compromised. Which S3 configuration provides the STRONGEST protection?
options: Enable S3 Versioning and MFA Delete on the logging bucket — MFA Delete requires the root account's MFA device for delete operations
Enable S3 Replication to a second region with S3 Object Lock — cross-region replication provides a backup copy that cannot be deleted if the primary is compromised
Enable S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode with a 90-day retention period — in Compliance mode, no user including the account root user can delete or modify objects before the retention period expires
Enable S3 Object Lock in Governance mode with a 90-day retention period — Governance mode allows users with special IAM permissions to override the lock if needed
explain: S3 Object Lock Compliance mode provides the strongest immutability guarantee in AWS S3. Once an object is locked in Compliance mode with a retention date, no AWS identity — including the account root user — can delete the object or change the retention period to an earlier date before expiration. Governance mode allows users with s3:BypassGovernanceRetention permission to override the lock, making it weaker. MFA Delete can be circumvented if root credentials and the MFA device are both compromised. Cross-region replication adds redundancy but does not add immutability to the primary bucket.

q: A data engineer needs to monitor an end-to-end data pipeline spanning Kinesis → Lambda → Glue → Redshift. When the pipeline has SLA breaches (end-to-end latency > 4 hours), the on-call engineer must be alerted within 5 minutes. Which monitoring approach provides the EARLIEST SLA breach detection?
options: Enable AWS X-Ray for all pipeline components — X-Ray calculates end-to-end latency and automatically fires an alarm when total trace duration exceeds 4 hours

Create CloudWatch alarms on individual service metrics (Kinesis GetRecords.IteratorAgeMilliseconds, Lambda Duration, Glue job duration) with SNS notification — each alarm fires when its service exceeds its expected time budget, catching SLA risk before end-to-end breach
Use Amazon CloudWatch Synthetics to run canary tests that simulate end-to-end data flow every 5 minutes
Check CloudWatch dashboards manually every hour — visual monitoring provides sufficient SLA oversight
explain: Proactive SLA monitoring requires alarms at each pipeline stage, not just at the end. By setting CloudWatch alarms on leading indicators — Kinesis IteratorAgeMilliseconds (consumer lag), Lambda P99 duration, Glue job elapsed time — the on-call engineer is alerted when any stage is running behind its expected time budget, before the 4-hour end-to-end SLA is breached. X-Ray does not support 4-hour traces (15-minute limit) and does not alarm on duration. CloudWatch Synthetics is for application availability testing.

q: A company ingests 10 TB of daily log files from on-premises servers to S3 using AWS DataSync. DataSync transfers use only 40% of the available 1 Gbps Direct Connect bandwidth. What DataSync configuration increases bandwidth utilization?
options: Enable DataSync scheduling to transfer files continuously instead of nightly — spreading transfers across 24 hours reduces peak bandwidth
Use AWS Snowball instead of DataSync — Snowball always uses 100% of available network bandwidth

Increase the DataSync task's parallel transfer workers — DataSync transfers multiple files concurrently. Increasing the number of worker threads allows DataSync to saturate available bandwidth with concurrent file transfers
Enable DataSync data compression — compressed transfers use less bandwidth but DataSync processes more files per second
explain: AWS DataSync uses multiple parallel threads to transfer files concurrently. The default worker configuration may not saturate the available network bandwidth, especially for workloads with many small files. Increasing the number of parallel transfer workers allows DataSync to open more simultaneous transfer streams, better utilizing the 1 Gbps Direct Connect capacity. DataSync compression can help but is more relevant for WAN optimization than bandwidth saturation. Continuous scheduling spreads load but doesn't fix the per-task bandwidth utilization.

q: Amazon Redshift has significantly degraded query performance after 3 months of heavy INSERT operations. EXPLAIN plans show full table scans where the optimizer previously used zone maps effectively. What maintenance operation MOST likely resolves this?
options: Run VACUUM REINDEX on all affected tables — REINDEX rebuilds the interleaved sort key indexes that degrade with heavy INSERT activity

Run VACUUM SORT ONLY followed by ANALYZE on the affected tables — VACUUM SORT restores the sort order of rows (which zone maps depend on), and ANALYZE updates column statistics for the query optimizer
Restart the Redshift cluster — a fresh start clears all internal state and restores zone map effectiveness
Disable result caching in the Redshift workgroup — cached results prevent the optimizer from using zone maps
explain: Redshift zone maps track min/max values for sort key columns in each 1 MB disk block. Zone maps are only effective when data is physically sorted. After extensive INSERT operations, new rows are appended to unsorted regions, fragmenting the sort order. VACUUM SORT ONLY physically re-sorts the unsorted rows into the sorted region, restoring tight zone map ranges. ANALYZE then updates column statistics so the optimizer makes correct decisions. REINDEX applies only to interleaved sort keys, not standard sort keys.

q: The security team requires that no S3 bucket in any member account can be made public, enforced across ALL member accounts without requiring individual account configuration. Which control achieves this?
options: Deploy an AWS Config rule in each member account that checks for public S3 buckets and sends findings to Security Hub
Create an SCP attached to the Organizations root that denies s3:PutBucketAcl for all public-acl conditions
Create IAM policies in each member account that deny s3:PutBucketPublicAccessBlock — this prevents anyone from disabling Block Public Access

Enable S3 Block Public Access at the AWS Organizations level — this setting applies to all existing and new S3 buckets in all member accounts under the organization, centrally enforced without individual account configuration
explain: AWS S3 Block Public Access can be configured at the AWS Organizations level. A data perimeter administrator can enable all four Block Public Access settings across all member accounts centrally. This setting cannot be overridden by individual account administrators or bucket owners. SCPs can restrict the ability to disable Block Public Access but cannot actively enable it. Config rules detect non-compliance but do not prevent the initial misconfiguration.

q: A Glue ETL job reads 500 million rows from S3 Parquet files and performs a GROUP BY aggregation. The job runs for 2 hours with 80% of time spent in the shuffle phase during aggregation. What Spark optimization MOST reduces shuffle time?
options: Increase spark.sql.shuffle.partitions from the default 200 to 2,000 — more shuffle partitions distribute data more evenly
Switch from Glue to EMR with dedicated shuffle service — EMR's external shuffle service is faster than Glue's built-in shuffle
Enable adaptive query execution (AQE) in Spark — AQE dynamically coalesces shuffle partitions and switches join strategies at runtime based on actual data statistics, reducing shuffle overhead
Increase Glue worker count from 10 to 100 — more workers process shuffle blocks in parallel, proportionally reducing shuffle time
explain: Spark Adaptive Query Execution (AQE, enabled by default in Spark 3.x) addresses the shuffle bottleneck in two key ways for GROUP BY aggregations: (1) it dynamically coalesces post-shuffle partitions — if many shuffle partitions are created but most are small, AQE merges them into fewer larger partitions, reducing task scheduling overhead; (2) it detects skewed partitions and splits them for parallel processing. For a GROUP BY on 500M rows, AQE often reduces shuffle time by 40-60% without configuration changes.

q: A data engineering team manages a Redshift cluster serving both an interactive BI dashboard (peak 9 AM - 5 PM) and a nightly batch load (midnight - 4 AM). The nightly batch degrades cluster performance during early morning hours when users start BI queries. How should WLM be configured?
options: Use Redshift scheduled actions to modify the WLM parameter group — swap queue memory allocations on a schedule: high memory for Batch queue at 11 PM, swap to high memory for BI queue at 6 AM
Create two WLM queues: a Batch queue and a BI queue. Manually switch queue priorities based on time of day using a Lambda function that calls the Redshift WLM API
Create a single WLM queue with auto WLM enabled — Redshift auto WLM dynamically assigns resources based on query type
Use Amazon EventBridge scheduled rules to pause and resume the Redshift cluster between batch and BI windows
explain: Redshift supports scheduled actions via the Redshift Scheduling API, which can modify cluster parameter groups on a cron schedule. Two WLM queues (Batch matched by IAM role, BI matched by application name) are configured with swapped memory allocations using scheduled modifications: at 11 PM the Batch queue gets 80% memory; at 6 AM the allocation reverses. This ensures batch jobs have maximum resources during the critical load window without manual intervention. Redshift does not support pausing/resuming non-Serverless clusters.

q: A data engineer needs to implement exactly-once semantics for a Kinesis consumer that writes aggregated records to Amazon DynamoDB. Network retries occasionally cause duplicate Lambda invocations for the same Kinesis sequence number. What pattern ensures each record is processed exactly once?
options: Set Lambda reserved concurrency to 1 for the Kinesis consumer — single-threaded processing prevents concurrent duplicate writes
Set the Kinesis event source mapping BisectBatchOnFunctionError to true — this ensures each record is processed in isolation without retry overlap

Include the Kinesis sequence number as the DynamoDB item's partition key and use a DynamoDB conditional write (attribute_not_exists) — if a duplicate invocation attempts to write the same sequence number, the conditional write fails silently
Use Kinesis enhanced fan-out — dedicated throughput eliminates duplicate deliveries from shared consumers
explain: Kinesis guarantees at-least-once delivery — retries and reprocessing are expected. To achieve effectively-exactly-once semantics in DynamoDB, each write must be idempotent. Using the Kinesis sequence number as the DynamoDB partition key and adding a conditional write expression (attribute_not_exists) ensures: first write succeeds; any retry with the same sequence number fails the condition check and is rejected silently. The Lambda function treats the ConditionalCheckFailedException as a successful no-op. Enhanced fan-out reduces lag but does not eliminate at-least-once redelivery.

q: A company stores sensitive research data in S3. The data lake is accessed by 50 Glue jobs, 20 Athena workgroups, and 5 Redshift Spectrum connections. A compliance requirement mandates all data access must be governed through a single control plane with column-level and row-level access policies. Which service provides this unified governance?
options: Amazon Macie — Macie classifies data sensitivity and applies access restrictions based on classification
AWS Config with custom rules — Config evaluates and enforces access policies across all services accessing S3

AWS Lake Formation — Lake Formation acts as a unified permission management layer over the Glue Data Catalog, enforcing column-level, row-level, and tag-based access policies for all integrated services from a single control plane
AWS IAM with resource-based policies — attach individual bucket policies controlling access per service
explain: AWS Lake Formation is purpose-built as a unified data access governance layer for AWS analytics services. It integrates with the Glue Data Catalog and intercepts data access from Athena, Glue ETL, Redshift Spectrum, and EMR — enforcing column-level security, row-level security, and tag-based access control from a single administrative interface. All 75 consumers are governed through Lake Formation permissions without modifying individual IAM policies or S3 bucket policies for each new service integration. IAM alone cannot enforce column or row-level filtering at query time.

q: A data engineer runs a PySpark job on Amazon EMR that reads 50 GB of data from S3 and performs a join between two datasets. The job fails with 'java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: GC overhead limit exceeded' on executor nodes. Memory usage shows executors are using 95% of heap. What is the FIRST adjustment to make?
options: Increase the number of EMR core nodes from 5 to 50 — more nodes reduce per-node data volume
Disable EMR's dynamic resource allocation — dynamic allocation reduces executor memory by reclaiming idle executors
Switch from PySpark to Scala Spark — Scala has lower JVM overhead and uses 30% less memory
Increase spark.executor.memory and spark.executor.memoryOverhead — if executors are at 95% heap, they need more memory allocation per executor and more off-heap buffer for shuffle operations
explain: When executor heap is at 95% and GC overhead limit is exceeded, the primary fix is to increase executor memory allocation. In EMR, this is controlled by spark.executor.memory (heap) and spark.executor.memoryOverhead (off-heap, used for Python processes, JVM overhead, and shuffle buffers). Increasing both gives each executor more headroom for the join operation. Simply adding nodes distributes the data but each executor still has the same memory limit. Disabling dynamic allocation and using Scala do not address per-executor memory limits.

q: A company migrates from a traditional data warehouse to Amazon Redshift. After migration, query performance is poor because most dimension joins cause cross-node data redistribution. What distribution strategy BEST minimizes redistribution for dimension joins in a star schema?
options: Use AUTO distribution and let Redshift automatically choose the best distribution for each table
Use EVEN distribution for all tables — EVEN distribution balances data and Redshift's optimizer handles redistribution automatically
Apply ALL distribution to all dimension tables under 10 million rows — ALL distribution replicates each dimension table to every node, making every dimension join local and eliminating redistribution
Use KEY distribution on all tables using the same join column — shared key columns ensure all tables are co-located
explain: In a star schema, fact tables contain billions of rows and dimension tables typically contain thousands to millions of rows. ALL distribution copies the entire dimension table to every compute node, ensuring that when a fact table row is processed on any node, the matching dimension row is available locally — eliminating cross-node redistribution. The rule of thumb is: use ALL for dimensions under 10M rows and KEY distribution on the fact table using the most common join key. EVEN distribution for dimensions creates cross-node joins for every query.

q: A data team runs daily Athena queries that apply different business logic against the same 500 GB base S3 dataset for 10 different reports. The team pays $2,500 per day in Athena costs (5 TB scanned × $5/TB). What architecture reduces cost most significantly?
options: Enable Athena Federated Query to route queries to DynamoDB — DynamoDB query costs are based on read capacity, not data scanned

Implement a pre-aggregation layer: run a single Glue job nightly that reads the 500 GB base dataset once and writes 10 pre-aggregated summary tables to S3 in Parquet format. Configure Athena to query the small summary tables instead of the raw 500 GB dataset
Use Amazon EMR with Apache Hive — Hive caches query results and reuses them for similar queries
Move the base dataset to Amazon Redshift and run all 10 reports as Redshift queries — Redshift charges per node-hour, not per byte scanned
explain: The 10 reports scan the same 500 GB base dataset with different logic — this is classic pre-aggregation territory. A single nightly Glue job reads the 500 GB once and writes 10 compact Parquet summary tables. Each summary table is typically a few MB to a few GB. The 10 subsequent Athena queries scan nearly zero data, reducing the $2,500/day Athena bill by ~99%. The Glue job cost at 10 G.1X workers for 30 minutes is approximately $0.44 — a massive ROI.

q: A data engineer needs to share specific tables from an Amazon Redshift cluster (Account A) with an analytics team in Account B using Redshift Data Sharing. The Account B team's Redshift cluster is in a different AWS region. What limitation must the engineer understand?
options: Use Redshift Spectrum with cross-account S3 access — Spectrum can read across regions without a Redshift cluster in Account B's region

Redshift Data Sharing does not natively support cross-region sharing. Both producer and consumer clusters must be in the same AWS region. For cross-region access, use Redshift cross-region snapshot copy or an ETL pipeline with cross-region S3 replication
Redshift Data Sharing supports cross-region sharing natively — no configuration is required beyond the standard datashare setup
Cross-region Redshift Data Sharing requires both clusters to use the same VPC — VPC peering between regions enables the data sharing network path
explain: Amazon Redshift Data Sharing requires producer and consumer Redshift clusters to be in the same AWS region. The live data-sharing network path does not traverse AWS regions. For cross-region data sharing use cases, alternatives include: Redshift cross-region snapshot copy (restores a copy of the cluster in the target region); ETL pipeline that exports data to S3 with cross-region replication; or cross-region S3 Data Lake with Lake Formation governance. Engineers should verify current AWS documentation as cross-region support may be added in future releases.

q: A streaming application uses Kinesis Data Streams with 8 shards. A business requirement emerges to double the pipeline's ingest capacity to 16 MB/s (currently 8 MB/s). What is the correct procedure to scale Kinesis?
options: Increase the Kinesis record size limit from 1 MB to 2 MB per record — larger records carry more data per PUT operation, effectively doubling throughput without adding shards
Enable Kinesis enhanced fan-out — enhanced fan-out increases consumer read throughput and indirectly allows producers to write more data
Add a second Kinesis Data Stream and split traffic at the producer level — one stream handles even partition keys, the other handles odd partition keys

Use the Kinesis UpdateShardCount API or console to increase shard count from 8 to 16 — Kinesis reshards the stream by splitting existing shards, doubling total throughput capacity to 16 MB/s. Active consumers automatically adapt to the new shard count
explain: Kinesis Data Streams throughput scales by adjusting shard count. Each shard supports 1 MB/s write throughput. To go from 8 MB/s to 16 MB/s, increase from 8 to 16 shards using the UpdateShardCount API. Kinesis performs a rolling reshard transparently. The Kinesis Producer Library (KPL) and Lambda event source mapping automatically discover new shards and begin consuming them — no code changes needed. The record size limit (1 MB per record) is a fixed service limit. Enhanced fan-out increases read throughput but has no effect on write throughput limits.

q: After several weeks of hourly Redshift COPY commands loading data from S3, the COPY command performance degrades significantly. A VACUUM SORT ONLY operation was run but performance did not improve. What else should the data engineer do?
options: Increase the Redshift cluster node count — more nodes parallelize the COPY command
Enable Redshift Auto Vacuum — Auto Vacuum handles all maintenance automatically, making manual VACUUM unnecessary

Run ANALYZE on the target table — COPY operations add rows that change column statistics, and stale statistics cause the Redshift optimizer to choose inefficient query plans for subsequent COPY-related operations
Run VACUUM DELETE ONLY — hourly COPY commands leave soft-deleted rows from previous loads that accumulate as tombstones
explain: Amazon Redshift's query optimizer uses column statistics generated by ANALYZE to choose efficient query plans. Repeated COPY operations add millions of rows, making existing statistics increasingly stale. The Redshift optimizer may choose poor join orders or inefficient strategies when statistics do not reflect the current data distribution. Running ANALYZE after large COPY operations updates statistics and restores optimizer accuracy. VACUUM SORT ONLY addressed sort order but not statistics. VACUUM DELETE ONLY reclaims space from deleted rows but does not update statistics.

q: A company must implement VPC endpoints for all AWS data services accessed by Glue and EMR jobs to ensure data never traverses the public internet. Services accessed include: S3, Glue Data Catalog, DynamoDB, and CloudWatch Logs. Which types of VPC endpoints are required for each?
options: All four services use Gateway endpoints — Gateway endpoints are the preferred type for all AWS data services
S3 uses a Gateway endpoint. DynamoDB, Glue, and CloudWatch Logs all use Interface endpoints
All four services use Interface endpoints (AWS PrivateLink) — Interface endpoints support all AWS managed services

S3 and DynamoDB use Gateway endpoints (free, route table-based). Glue and CloudWatch Logs use Interface endpoints (hourly + data charge, ENI-based). Configure the appropriate endpoint type for each service
explain: AWS VPC endpoints come in two types: Gateway endpoints (S3 and DynamoDB only) are free, configured in VPC route tables, and redirect traffic to AWS's internal network. Interface endpoints (AWS PrivateLink) create ENIs in your subnet and apply to all other AWS services including Glue Data Catalog, CloudWatch Logs, Kinesis, etc. — incurring hourly charges plus data processing fees. For Glue and EMR jobs: S3 → Gateway endpoint, DynamoDB → Gateway endpoint, Glue API/Catalog → Interface endpoint, CloudWatch Logs → Interface endpoint.

q: A data engineer needs to implement a pipeline where the output of a Glue streaming job must be validated before being made available for querying in Athena. If validation fails, the output partition must be quarantined. Which orchestration pattern implements this MOST cleanly?
options: Use Step Functions to orchestrate: (1) Glue streaming job; (2) validation job; (3) on Pass → move output to the Athena-queryable prefix; on Fail → move output to quarantine prefix and send SNS alert
Add validation logic inside the Glue streaming job — if validation fails, the job writes to a quarantine prefix instead of the main prefix
Use S3 Event Notifications to trigger a Lambda validation function after the Glue job writes to S3 — Lambda validates and deletes non-conforming files
Use Glue triggers to chain the streaming job with a validation job — Glue triggers automatically quarantine failed output
explain: Step Functions provides the cleanest separation of concerns for conditional post-processing workflows. The state machine starts the Glue job, runs the validation Lambda/Glue job, then uses a Choice state to evaluate the validation result — success routes to a Task state that moves output to the Athena-queryable prefix; failure routes to quarantine and triggers an SNS alert. Athena is configured to query only the approved prefix. This keeps business logic in the orchestration layer, not embedded in the Glue job itself.

q: A company migrates a Teradata data warehouse to Amazon Redshift. The Teradata schema uses Primary Index (PI) for data distribution and Partition Primary Index (PPI) for partition-based pruning. What is the EQUIVALENT Redshift design for a 5-billion-row fact table primarily queried with date range filters and frequently joined on customer_id?
options: Use AUTO distribution and an interleaved sort key on both customer_id and transaction_date — interleaved sort keys equally weight multiple query patterns

Use KEY distribution on customer_id (equivalent to PI) and sort key on transaction_date (equivalent to PPI) — KEY distribution collocates rows with the same customer_id for efficient joins, while the sort key enables zone map-based partition pruning for date range queries
Use ALL distribution on the fact table to ensure all data is local for joins
Use EVEN distribution and no sort key — Redshift's Adaptive Query Execution handles the rest
explain: Mapping Teradata PI and PPI to Redshift: (1) Primary Index → Redshift DISTKEY — the distribution key co-locates rows with the same customer_id for local joins without redistribution; (2) PPI → Redshift sort key — with transaction_date as the sort key, Redshift zone maps skip entire disk blocks outside a date range filter, equivalent to PPI partition pruning. ALL distribution on a 5B-row table is infeasible. Interleaved sort keys have higher VACUUM cost and are less effective for date range queries than a simple sort key.

q: An EMR cluster processes genomics data containing PHI. The cluster must ensure that all inter-node communication (Spark shuffle traffic between executors) is encrypted in transit. Which EMR configuration enables this?
options: Enable Enhanced VPC Routing — Enhanced VPC Routing routes inter-node traffic through the VPC and encrypts it at the network layer
Create an EMR cluster in a private subnet — private subnets encrypt all inter-node traffic using VPC network ACLs
Enable EMR in-transit encryption by creating an EMR security configuration with EnableInTransitEncryption: true and providing a TLS certificate for Spark inter-node communication
Enable EMR at-rest encryption using a KMS key — at-rest encryption also protects in-transit inter-node data
explain: EMR security configurations control encryption settings for the cluster. Setting InTransitEncryptionConfiguration with EnableInTransitEncryption: true and providing a certificate provider enables TLS encryption for HDFS data node transfer, Spark shuffle data between executor nodes, and Presto/Hive inter-node communication. Private subnets and VPC network ACLs operate at the network packet level but do not encrypt application-layer shuffle data. At-rest encryption only protects data stored on disk. Enhanced VPC Routing routes S3 traffic through VPC endpoints but does not encrypt intra-cluster Spark traffic.

q: A data pipeline uses AWS Glue to read from S3 and write results to an S3 bucket in a different AWS account (Account B). The Glue job runs in Account A. The job fails with 'Access Denied' when writing to Account B's bucket despite the Account A Glue role having s3:PutObject in its IAM policy. What is MISSING?
options: Account B must add Account A as a trusted account in IAM Organizations
Account A must create a VPC endpoint for S3 to allow cross-account writes

Account B's S3 bucket must have a bucket policy that explicitly grants s3:PutObject to Account A's Glue IAM role ARN — cross-account S3 writes require both identity-based allow AND resource-based allow (Account B bucket policy)
Glue cannot write to cross-account S3 buckets — use S3 Replication instead
explain: Cross-account S3 access requires permissions from BOTH sides: (1) the IAM role in Account A must have s3:PutObject in its identity-based policy (already configured); (2) the S3 bucket in Account B must have a resource-based bucket policy explicitly allowing the Account A Glue IAM role ARN to perform s3:PutObject. Without the bucket policy grant in Account B, AWS denies the request because there is no resource-based trust from Account B's side. This is a fundamental AWS cross-account access pattern.

q: A company stores product catalog data in Amazon DynamoDB (500K items, 2 KB avg). A nightly Glue job reads all 500K items to generate analytics reports. The DynamoDB table is provisioned at 1,000 RCU and the Glue job throttles on DynamoDB reads. What is the BEST solution that avoids impacting the production DynamoDB table?
options: Increase DynamoDB provisioned RCU to 10,000 for the duration of the Glue job — higher RCU eliminates throttling
Use DynamoDB Parallel Scan with configurable read rate limiting — set Limit parameter to throttle Glue reads

Use Amazon DynamoDB Export to S3 feature (using PITR) to export the table once per day — the Glue job reads from S3 instead of scanning the live DynamoDB table, completely offloading analytics reads from production
Use DynamoDB on-demand capacity mode — on-demand scales automatically to handle the Glue scan
explain: DynamoDB Export to S3 (using PITR) exports the full table contents to S3 without consuming any read capacity units from the table and without impacting production workloads. The export runs against a consistent snapshot. The Glue analytics job then reads from S3, completely decoupling analytics reads from production DynamoDB capacity. This is the AWS-recommended pattern for analytics on DynamoDB data at scale. Increasing RCU or switching to on-demand still consumes table read capacity during the scan and can impact production latency.

q: A data engineer builds a pipeline where a Lambda function reads sensitive customer records from S3 and writes enriched records to another S3 bucket. The security team requires that the S3 data never leaves the AWS network. The Lambda currently runs without a VPC configuration. What is the REQUIRED architecture change?
options: Enable S3 server-side encryption — encrypted S3 transfers are automatically routed through AWS private backbone

Deploy the Lambda function inside a VPC and create S3 Gateway endpoints in the VPC's route table — S3 Gateway endpoints route S3 traffic from the Lambda ENI directly to S3 over AWS's private network without traversing the public internet
Add a VPC endpoint for Lambda — Lambda VPC endpoints route Lambda invocation traffic through private networks, indirectly routing S3 access through the VPC
Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on both buckets — accelerated transfers route through AWS's backbone network
explain: By default, Lambda functions (even those not in a VPC) communicate with S3 via HTTPS over the public internet. To ensure S3 traffic stays within AWS's private network: (1) configure the Lambda function to run inside a VPC; (2) create an S3 Gateway endpoint in the VPC and add it to the route table for the Lambda's subnets. S3 Gateway endpoints are free and redirect all S3 API traffic over AWS's private network. Transfer Acceleration optimizes global upload speed but doesn't change network routing. SSE controls data-at-rest encryption, not network routing.

q: A data engineer schedules an Amazon Redshift COPY command to load data from S3 into a fact table every hour. After several weeks, a CloudWatch alarm fires because the 'DatabaseConnections' metric on an RDS MySQL source hits 590+, causing Glue jobs to fail with 'connection refused'. What is the BEST architectural fix?
options: Enable RDS Multi-AZ — Multi-AZ adds a standby instance that handles excess connections during peak hours
Add a Read Replica and route Glue jobs to the replica — read replicas have separate connection limits

Deploy Amazon RDS Proxy in front of the RDS MySQL instance — RDS Proxy maintains a connection pool to RDS and efficiently multiplexes thousands of application connections onto a smaller pool of database connections, preventing connection exhaustion
Increase the RDS instance to db.r5.4xlarge — larger instances support more concurrent connections
explain: RDS Proxy is purpose-built for connection pooling and multiplexing. Multiple Glue worker threads each open their own JDBC connection to RDS, quickly exhausting the max_connections limit. RDS Proxy accepts all Glue connections and multiplexes them onto a managed pool of 10-20 actual database connections, effectively eliminating the max_connections bottleneck. Upgrading the instance class increases max_connections but not proportionally — a heavy Glue workload can exhaust even a large instance. Read replicas work for read-only jobs but don't solve write connection exhaustion.

q: A security analyst needs to query CloudTrail logs across all 20 accounts from a single location using Athena. What is the MOST OPERATIONALLY EFFICIENT setup?
options: Use CloudWatch Logs Insights — CloudTrail integrates with CloudWatch Logs natively across all accounts without S3 configuration
Create separate CloudTrail trails in each of the 20 accounts and query each account's S3 bucket individually using Athena cross-account queries

Create an AWS Organizations CloudTrail trail from the management account — this single trail captures API events from all member accounts and delivers logs to a central S3 bucket. Create an Athena table over the central bucket to query all accounts' events from one location
Deploy a Lambda function in each account that aggregates CloudTrail logs to a central account using cross-account S3 replication
explain: AWS Organizations CloudTrail (organization trail) is created once from the management account and automatically applies to all current and future member accounts. All accounts' CloudTrail events are delivered to a single central S3 bucket. Creating a single Athena table partitioned by account_id and region over this central bucket enables analysts to query all 20 accounts' API activity from one Athena workspace. This eliminates the operational overhead of managing 20 separate trails. CloudWatch Logs Insights queries CloudWatch Logs, not S3 — not all CloudTrail events go to CloudWatch Logs by default.

q: A data engineer needs to process CDC events from Amazon DynamoDB Streams using AWS Lambda. The Lambda function enriches each record with data from an external API and writes to S3. During traffic spikes, the Lambda function falls behind the stream and the iterator age grows beyond 1 hour. What is the MOST EFFECTIVE way to increase stream processing throughput?
options: Enable DynamoDB Streams enhanced fan-out — dedicated throughput per consumer reduces iterator age independently of other consumers

Increase the Lambda event source mapping batch size and enable parallelization factor (up to 10) — parallelization factor allows multiple concurrent Lambda invocations per shard, increasing throughput proportionally without changing the DynamoDB Streams configuration
Switch from Lambda to Kinesis Data Streams — Kinesis supports higher throughput than DynamoDB Streams
Reduce Lambda memory from 512 MB to 128 MB — lower memory allocation increases the number of concurrent Lambda invocations allowed
explain: Lambda event source mapping for DynamoDB Streams supports a parallelization factor of 1–10 per shard. With parallelization factor=10, Lambda creates up to 10 concurrent invocations per DynamoDB Streams shard, each processing a different batch of records from the same shard. This multiplies throughput by up to 10x without any DynamoDB or stream configuration changes. Combined with a larger batch size (up to 10,000 records), this dramatically reduces iterator age during traffic spikes. DynamoDB Streams does not support enhanced fan-out (that is a Kinesis feature). Reducing Lambda memory lowers per-invocation cost but does not increase concurrent invocations — Lambda concurrency is not memory-bound.

q: A data engineering team at a healthcare company needs to restrict analysts from viewing patient SSN and date-of-birth columns in an Amazon Athena table backed by S3. The solution must be centrally managed without modifying individual S3 bucket policies. Which approach achieves this?
options: Create a separate Athena view per analyst that excludes the sensitive columns from SELECT statements
Apply S3 bucket policies that deny GetObject for specific column names in the data files
Encrypt SSN and DOB columns with different KMS keys and withhold the key ARNs from analyst IAM roles
Use AWS Lake Formation column-level security to grant analysts access only to permitted columns, enforced automatically when querying through Athena
explain: AWS Lake Formation supports column-level security (fine-grained access control) that lets administrators specify which columns each IAM principal can access. When analysts query Athena, Lake Formation intercepts the request and filters out restricted columns automatically. S3 bucket policies operate at the object level and cannot restrict individual columns. Separate views require manual maintenance per analyst. KMS per-column encryption is not a native feature and would not prevent reading encrypted ciphertext.

q: A data platform team uses Amazon S3 as a data lake and wants to enforce table-level access control so that different business units can only query the tables relevant to their domain. The team finds that S3 bucket policies alone are insufficient because multiple tables share the same S3 prefix. Which AWS service provides centralized table-level permission management for the data lake?
options: Amazon Macie classification labels applied per table to restrict consumer access
AWS Glue Data Catalog resource policies that enumerate allowed tables per IAM role
AWS IAM resource-based policies applied directly to each S3 prefix that maps to a table
AWS Lake Formation table-level permissions that supersede S3 bucket policies and control access per Glue Data Catalog table
explain: AWS Lake Formation provides centralized, fine-grained access control at the database and table level within the Glue Data Catalog. Lake Formation permissions take precedence over S3 bucket policies for services that integrate with it (Athena, Redshift Spectrum, EMR). This solves the shared-prefix problem because access is enforced at the logical table boundary, not the S3 object path. IAM resource policies and Glue resource policies do not provide the same depth of integration. Macie is a data discovery and classification service, not an access control mechanism.

q: A data engineer ingests JSON records from an e-commerce API where each order contains a nested array of line items. The pipeline must flatten the data so each line item becomes its own row in a Parquet file stored in Amazon S3. The engineer uses AWS Glue ETL. Which Glue transformation approach handles nested arrays and struct fields most efficiently without writing custom parsing code?
options: Use a Glue DynamicFrame with the Relationalize transform to automatically flatten nested structs and arrays into separate DataFrames that can be joined and written as flat Parquet
Convert the nested JSON to CSV first using a Lambda function, then load the flat CSV into Glue for transformation
Load the JSON directly into Amazon Redshift using COPY and let Redshift handle the nested array flattening via the SUPER data type
Read the JSON files with a Glue Job using boto3 json.loads(), iterate the array in Python, and write individual rows to S3 using the Parquet library
explain: AWS Glue DynamicFrames support the Relationalize transform, which automatically detects nested structs and arrays and produces a set of flat DataFrames. Arrays are exploded so each element becomes its own row, and struct fields are promoted to top-level columns. This eliminates the need to write custom parsing code. Writing custom Python loops with boto3 is error-prone and does not leverage Glue's distributed execution. Pre-converting to CSV loses the array structure and adds an unnecessary Lambda step. Redshift SUPER is for querying nested data in Redshift, not for transforming JSON to flat Parquet in S3.

q: A financial services company streams transaction records from thousands of ATMs into Amazon Kinesis Data Streams. Downstream consumers fail silently when records arrive with unexpected field types or missing required fields. The data engineering team wants to catch schema violations as early as possible and track the failure rate over time. Which design best meets these requirements?
options: Add a post-processing Glue job that runs nightly to scan S3 for records that failed to load and report violations in a weekly email
Enable Kinesis Data Streams server-side encryption so that malformed records are automatically discarded before reaching consumers
Let downstream Lambda consumers write failed records to an SQS dead-letter queue and manually inspect the DLQ weekly to identify schema issues

Apply schema validation at the Kinesis producer using AWS Glue Schema Registry before records are published; reject invalid records, emit a CloudWatch metric for each rejection, and route violating records to a separate S3 error prefix
explain: Validating at the producer with AWS Glue Schema Registry enforces the schema before invalid data enters the stream, preventing bad records from propagating to downstream consumers. Emitting CloudWatch metrics per rejection enables real-time monitoring and alerting on failure rates. Routing violations to an S3 error prefix enables forensic review without blocking valid traffic. A DLQ-only approach is reactive and lacks real-time visibility. Server-side encryption has no effect on data validity or schema conformance. Nightly post-processing introduces long latency before issues are discovered.

q: A data engineering team uses AWS Lambda functions to transform records fetched from Amazon DynamoDB Streams and write the results to Amazon S3. During peak hours the function handles 500 concurrent invocations, but the team observes high latency and throttling errors. Profiling shows that 40% of execution time is spent initializing the AWS SDK and loading a 15 MB reference dataset on every cold start. Which combination of optimizations best reduces cold start impact and throttling?
options: Increase the Lambda function timeout to 15 minutes and set reserved concurrency to 1000 to allow more simultaneous executions

Move the SDK initialization and reference dataset load outside the handler function into the global scope; set provisioned concurrency to pre-warm instances; and package the reference dataset as a Lambda Layer
Switch from DynamoDB Streams to SQS FIFO queues so that Lambda processes records sequentially, eliminating concurrency and cold starts entirely
Deploy the transformation logic to AWS Fargate and trigger it from an EventBridge rule every 5 minutes to batch-process DynamoDB Stream records
explain: Moving SDK initialization and static dataset loading to the global scope (outside the handler) means those operations run once per container instance and are reused across warm invocations. Lambda Layers let the reference dataset be versioned and shared, keeping the deployment package small. Provisioned concurrency pre-warms a pool of execution environments, eliminating cold starts for predictable peak loads. Increasing timeout does not reduce initialization cost. Sequential SQS FIFO processing sacrifices throughput and does not eliminate cold starts. Fargate adds operational overhead and introduces polling latency incompatible with near-real-time stream processing.

q: A data lake on Amazon S3 stores 200 TB of clickstream data originally ingested as gzip-compressed JSON files. Analysts run frequent aggregation queries in Amazon Athena filtering on event_date and event_type. Query scan times are slow and costs are high. The data engineering team wants to re-format the dataset to maximize Athena query performance and minimize data scanned per query. Which storage format and partitioning strategy should they use?
options: Convert to gzip CSV files and partition by event_date, because CSV is widely supported and gzip reduces file size significantly
Convert to Apache Parquet with Snappy compression, partition the data by event_date and event_type in S3, and run MSCK REPAIR TABLE to register partitions in the Glue Data Catalog
Keep the original gzip JSON but increase the Athena query timeout setting to allow longer scans
Load the JSON into Amazon DynamoDB with event_date as the partition key, then query DynamoDB from Athena using the DynamoDB connector
explain: Apache Parquet is a columnar format that allows Athena to read only the columns needed for a query rather than entire rows. Snappy compression provides a good balance of compression ratio and CPU decompression speed. Partitioning by event_date and event_type enables Athena partition pruning, which skips S3 prefixes that do not match the WHERE clause, dramatically reducing data scanned and query cost. gzip JSON is a row-based format requiring full row scans. Increasing timeout does not reduce data scanned. DynamoDB is an OLTP database not designed for analytical aggregation workloads at this scale.

q: A multi-tenant analytics platform stores all customers' data in shared Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL tables, with a tenant_id column identifying each customer's rows. Analysts must only see rows belonging to their tenant. The security team requires this enforcement at the database level so that no application bug can accidentally expose another tenant's data. Which database feature enforces row-level isolation transparently for every query?
options: Add a WHERE tenant_id = :current_tenant clause to every application query and conduct quarterly code reviews to verify compliance
Use PostgreSQL Row-Level Security (RLS) policies that automatically filter rows based on the current database session's tenant context, enforced by the database engine for every query
Enable RDS encryption at rest with tenant-specific KMS keys so that rows belonging to different tenants are encrypted with different keys
Create a separate RDS instance per tenant so that database-level isolation prevents any cross-tenant row access
explain: PostgreSQL Row-Level Security (RLS) allows administrators to define policies that the database engine enforces automatically on SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations. When a session sets its tenant context (e.g., via SET app.tenant_id), the RLS policy applies a predicate that restricts all queries to matching rows regardless of what the application sends. This eliminates the risk of a missing WHERE clause exposing other tenants' data. Separate RDS instances are expensive and operationally complex. Application-level WHERE clauses rely on developers not making mistakes. Encryption at rest does not enforce query-time row isolation.

q: An IoT platform ingests telemetry from 50,000 industrial sensors via Amazon Kinesis Data Streams. Due to unreliable network conditions, sensors occasionally retransmit the same reading multiple times within a 10-minute window. Downstream analytics must count each physical reading exactly once. The engineering team wants to deduplicate within the stream processing layer without running a separate batch deduplication job. Which approach achieves exactly-once counting most effectively?
options: Use Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose to buffer records for 5 minutes before delivery to S3, which automatically deduplicates records during the buffering window
Increase the Kinesis shard count to reduce the likelihood that duplicate records land on the same shard, making duplicates rare enough to ignore
Enable Kinesis enhanced fan-out on the consumer shard to receive records faster and skip processing any record received within 1 second of a previous record from the same sensor

Assign a deterministic record key combining sensor_id and timestamp_bucket so Kinesis routes all readings from the same sensor in the same time bucket to the same shard, then use a consumer-side deduplication map keyed on sensor_id + timestamp_bucket with a 10-minute TTL
explain: Assigning a record key of sensor_id + timestamp_bucket guarantees that all transmissions of the same reading are routed to the same shard and processed by the same consumer instance. The consumer maintains a small in-memory or ElastiCache-backed deduplication map with a TTL matching the retransmission window (10 minutes), so duplicate records are detected and discarded before counting. Enhanced fan-out improves throughput but does not deduplicate. Increasing shard count distributes duplicates across more shards, making deduplication harder. Kinesis Data Firehose does not deduplicate records during buffering.

q: A media streaming company ingests viewer event data into Amazon Kinesis Data Streams. Traffic spikes 10x during live sports events, causing the stream to become under-sharded and records to be throttled. The team wants the stream to scale shard capacity automatically in response to traffic without manual intervention. Which Kinesis feature enables automatic shard scaling?
options: Switch to Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose, which has no shard limits and scales automatically to handle any ingest rate
Set a CloudWatch alarm on IncomingBytes to trigger a Lambda function that calls UpdateShardCount whenever throughput exceeds 80% of current capacity
Enable Kinesis Data Streams On-Demand mode, which automatically scales shard capacity up and down based on actual throughput without requiring manual shard management
Enable Kinesis enhanced fan-out, which automatically adds shards when throughput exceeds the per-shard limit of 1 MB/s
explain: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams On-Demand mode automatically manages shard capacity in response to changing traffic patterns, scaling up to handle bursts and scaling down during low traffic. It eliminates the need to pre-provision shards or manually trigger UpdateShardCount. This is the native AWS solution for variable-rate stream ingestion. Enhanced fan-out increases read throughput for consumers but does not add shards or increase write capacity. Kinesis Data Firehose is a delivery service, not a real-time stream processing solution with the same consumer API. A CloudWatch-triggered Lambda using UpdateShardCount introduces lag and complexity compared to On-Demand mode.

q: A healthcare data pipeline ingests patient intake forms containing names, dates of birth, and diagnosis codes from hospital systems into Amazon S3 for analytics. Regulations require that PHI fields (name, DOB) are masked before data reaches the analytics layer. The security team mandates that masking happens at ingestion time, not in downstream jobs. Which AWS approach applies data masking during the ingestion stage?
options: Store raw data in S3 with SSE-KMS encryption and rely on IAM policies to prevent analytics users from decrypting the PHI fields after ingestion
Configure an S3 Object Lambda function that intercepts GET requests from analytics users and removes PHI fields on the fly during reads
Enable Amazon Macie on the S3 bucket to automatically detect and redact PHI fields after the raw files are stored
Use an AWS Glue ETL job in the ingestion pipeline that applies a custom transform to hash or tokenize the PHI fields before writing the masked records to the analytics S3 prefix
explain: An AWS Glue ETL job positioned in the ingestion pipeline can apply custom transformations—such as SHA-256 hashing, tokenization, or format-preserving encryption—to PHI columns before the records are written to the analytics S3 prefix. This ensures masked data is stored at rest and PHI never reaches the analytics layer. Encryption with KMS protects data at rest but does not mask field values; an authorized analytics user with the KMS key sees plaintext PHI. Amazon Macie detects and classifies sensitive data but does not redact or modify files. S3 Object Lambda can mask on read but the raw PHI is still stored unmasked in S3, violating the at-ingestion requirement.

q: A company streams IoT sensor data from 10,000 devices at 100 records/second each (1 million records/second total). The data must be stored in Amazon S3 in Parquet format for analytics and also delivered to Amazon OpenSearch for real-time dashboards within 10 seconds. Which architecture handles both requirements?
options: Use Amazon SQS to queue all device messages, trigger a Lambda function per message to convert to Parquet and write to S3, and separately trigger another Lambda to index into OpenSearch

Use Amazon Kinesis Data Streams to ingest all device data, connect two consumers: (1) Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose for buffered S3 delivery with Parquet conversion, and (2) a Lambda function that indexes records into OpenSearch in near-real-time
Use Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose directly for all ingestion — configure a single Firehose stream with parallel delivery to both S3 (Parquet) and OpenSearch
Write all device data directly to Amazon DynamoDB Streams, use a Lambda consumer for real-time OpenSearch indexing, and use DynamoDB export to S3 for Parquet conversion daily
explain: Kinesis Data Streams handles 1M records/second ingestion and supports multiple independent consumers. Consumer 1 (Firehose) buffers records, converts to Parquet using AWS Glue schema, and delivers to S3 — meeting the batch analytics requirement. Consumer 2 (Lambda) processes records with low latency and indexes into OpenSearch within seconds — meeting the real-time dashboard requirement. Kinesis Firehose alone cannot deliver to both S3 and OpenSearch from a single stream with different processing. SQS with per-message Lambda at 1M records/second would be extremely expensive and face concurrency limits. DynamoDB Streams has a maximum of 2 readers and daily export doesn't meet real-time requirements.

q: A data engineering team uses AWS Glue to transform raw CSV files in S3 to Parquet. During processing, Glue jobs fail intermittently with 'OutOfMemoryError' when processing large files (10GB+). Which approach MOST effectively resolves this without significantly increasing cost?
options: Enable Glue job bookmarks and dynamic frame pushdown predicates to process large files incrementally, and use Glue's automatic worker scaling — also consider switching from Standard to G.2X worker type for memory-intensive workloads
Increase the number of DPU (Data Processing Units) workers to 100 to ensure enough memory is available for all file sizes
Pre-split all large CSV files to under 1GB using an S3 event-triggered Lambda before Glue processes them
Move all processing to AWS Lambda with 10GB memory configuration to handle large file transformations without Spark overhead
explain: Glue's OutOfMemoryError on large files usually means the Spark partition holding the file data is too large for worker memory. Solutions: (1) G.2X workers have 2x memory of standard G.1X workers — better for memory-intensive operations. (2) Pushdown predicates reduce data read. (3) Job bookmarks enable incremental processing so previously processed data is skipped. (4) Dynamic frame operations (like relational toDF()) use Spark's distributed processing to split large files across multiple workers. Simply adding 100 DPUs increases cost proportionally without fixing the memory-per-worker issue. Lambda has a 10GB memory limit, 15-minute timeout, and 512MB-10GB /tmp storage — insufficient for 10GB+ file processing. Pre-splitting helps but adds pipeline complexity.

q: A company ingests customer transaction data into Amazon S3 via Kinesis Data Firehose. A downstream analytics team reports that query performance on Amazon Athena is poor because data is stored in small files (1-5MB each) across many S3 prefixes. What is the ROOT CAUSE and the MOST effective fix?
options: Enable Athena query result caching with a 24-hour TTL to avoid re-scanning the same small files for repeated queries
The S3 prefix structure is causing S3 request throttling — change the prefix to use random prefixes (hash-based) instead of date-based prefixes

Kinesis Firehose's buffering configuration creates many small files — increase buffer size (up to 128MB) and buffer interval (up to 900 seconds) in the Firehose delivery stream to create larger files; also consider converting to Parquet format and enabling S3 Intelligent-Tiering for older partitions
Athena's query performance is poor due to insufficient concurrency — request a service quota increase for parallel query execution
explain: The 'small files problem' is a classic data lake performance issue. Kinesis Firehose default buffering (5MB / 5 minutes) creates many small files. Each Athena query must open and read each file individually — high S3 API overhead per file. The fix: increase Firehose buffer size (up to 128MB) and interval (up to 900 seconds) to create fewer, larger files. Converting to Parquet adds columnar compression (~10x file size reduction) and predicate pushdown. This reduces both S3 API calls and data scanned. Athena concurrency affects parallel query throughput, not single-query performance on small files. S3 random prefixes help with high-throughput PUT operations, not read performance. Query caching helps for repeated identical queries but doesn't fix the underlying file size problem.

q: A company receives daily batch files from partners in various formats: CSV, JSON, XML, and Avro. All files must be normalized into a unified Parquet schema in Amazon S3. The transformation logic is complex, requiring multi-step joins and aggregations. Which AWS service handles this MOST cost-effectively for daily batch processing?
options: AWS Lambda functions triggered by S3 events for each file format, with separate Lambda functions per format type connected via SQS for the join operations
Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose with a data transformation Lambda to normalize formats and convert to Parquet before S3 delivery
Amazon EMR with a custom Hadoop MapReduce job for format normalization, running on a persistent cluster to handle daily files

AWS Glue with a PySpark ETL job that reads all input formats (Glue natively supports CSV, JSON, XML, Avro), applies the transformation logic using Spark operations, and writes the output as Parquet to S3 — serverless, pay-per-DPU-second, no infrastructure management
explain: AWS Glue is the optimal choice for daily batch ETL with complex transformations: (1) Native support for all mentioned formats (CSV, JSON, XML, Avro) via Glue classifiers and DynamicFrames. (2) PySpark enables complex multi-step joins and aggregations at scale. (3) Serverless — no cluster management, billed per DPU-second (cost-effective for batch). (4) Built-in Parquet output support. Amazon EMR with persistent clusters is more expensive for daily batch workloads. Lambda's 15-minute timeout and 10GB memory limit are insufficient for complex multi-file joins and aggregations. Firehose with Lambda transformation has payload size limits (6MB) and is designed for streaming, not complex batch joins.

q: A data lake stores 50TB of historical analytics data in Amazon S3. Data older than 1 year is rarely accessed but must be retained for 7 years for compliance. Frequently accessed data (last 30 days) must have query response times under 2 seconds. Which S3 storage strategy minimizes cost while meeting performance requirements?
options: Store all data in S3 Standard to ensure consistent performance across all time periods, accepting the higher storage cost as a compliance requirement

Configure S3 Intelligent-Tiering for frequently accessed data (last 30 days prefix) to automatically manage active/infrequent tiers, and use an S3 Lifecycle policy to transition data older than 1 year to S3 Glacier Deep Archive for minimum cost retention
Use Amazon EFS with provisioned throughput for frequently accessed data and S3 Glacier for historical data, migrating data between tiers daily using AWS DataSync
Store all data in S3 One Zone-IA to reduce storage costs while meeting the retention requirement — the single-AZ storage is sufficient for analytics data
explain: A tiered storage strategy matches cost to access patterns. S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between Frequent and Infrequent Access tiers based on actual access patterns — no manual management for the hot tier. An S3 Lifecycle policy automatically transitions data after 1 year to Glacier Deep Archive (the cheapest tier at $0.00099/GB/month), achieving compliance retention at minimum cost. S3 Standard for 50TB x 7 years is unnecessarily expensive. EFS adds operational complexity and higher cost than S3. S3 One Zone-IA reduces availability (single AZ) and recovery from accidental deletion is limited — inappropriate for compliance-critical data.

q: A company stores customer data in Amazon Redshift. A data governance policy requires that all personally identifiable information (PII) columns (email, phone, SSN) be masked for all users except the data privacy team. Which Amazon Redshift feature implements this with LEAST operational overhead?
options: Create database views for each table that replace PII columns with masked values, grant all users access to the views only, and grant the privacy team direct table access

Use Amazon Redshift dynamic data masking — define masking policies for PII columns that return masked values (e.g., 'XXX-XX-XXXX' for SSN) for all roles except the privacy team role, which receives unmasked values
Export data to Amazon S3, use AWS Glue to create a masked version of the dataset, and load the masked version back into a separate Redshift schema for regular users
Use AWS Lake Formation column-level security to restrict access to PII columns in Redshift tables for unauthorized users
explain: Amazon Redshift dynamic data masking is purpose-built for this requirement. Masking policies define how column values are transformed based on the querying user's role — regular users see masked values while the privacy team sees actual data. This is enforced at the SQL engine level, transparent to application code. Views per table require creating and maintaining N×M view/table combinations — operational overhead. AWS Lake Formation column security works for S3/Glue-based data but isn't the native Redshift mechanism. Creating a separate masked schema requires ETL processes and duplicates storage.

q: A company's Amazon Redshift cluster shows consistently high query latency for reports that join a 10TB fact table with 50 dimension tables. The fact table was loaded without distribution keys or sort keys. Which optimization provides the GREATEST performance improvement?
options: Enable Redshift Concurrency Scaling to add additional cluster capacity when queries are waiting, which reduces overall queue time for all queries
Convert all dimension tables to use DISTSTYLE ALL so every node has a full copy, eliminating network traffic for star schema joins
Enable Redshift Automatic Table Optimization (ATO) and wait 24 hours — ATO will automatically add optimal distribution and sort keys based on query patterns

Choose an appropriate DISTKEY on the fact table's most frequently joined column (e.g., customer_id), set the same DISTKEY on the large dimension tables, and define SORTKEY on the most frequently filtered columns — this eliminates data redistribution during joins and enables zone map pruning
explain: For a star schema with a large fact table and multiple dimension tables, the key optimization is distribution alignment. Setting the same DISTKEY (e.g., customer_id) on both the fact table and large dimension tables ensures that matching rows co-locate on the same Redshift nodes — eliminating expensive data redistribution (broadcast) during joins. SORTKEY on filter columns enables zone map optimization (skip irrelevant disk blocks). Concurrency Scaling adds capacity for concurrent queries but doesn't reduce individual query latency. DISTSTYLE ALL on large dimension tables would be very expensive (50 tables × N nodes). ATO can help long-term but requires workload analysis time and doesn't provide immediate relief.

q: A data team runs Amazon Athena queries on a data lake in S3. Queries on partitioned tables take 30-60 seconds even with small result sets because Athena needs to list thousands of S3 prefixes to identify the right partitions. Which optimization reduces partition discovery time?
options: Convert the data from Parquet to CSV format — CSV files don't require partition metadata discovery before scanning
Increase the number of Athena DPUs by requesting a quota increase — more parallel readers reduce the time to list partitions across S3
Use Amazon Redshift Spectrum instead of Athena — Spectrum automatically builds partition caches that eliminate the discovery overhead

Enable partition indexing in the AWS Glue Data Catalog for the table — Glue partition indexes store partition metadata in a B-tree structure, allowing Athena to find the correct partitions directly without listing all S3 prefixes
explain: With thousands of partitions, Athena's default behavior of listing all S3 prefixes to find relevant partitions creates significant overhead. AWS Glue partition indexing creates a metadata index (B-tree) that allows Athena to directly look up partitions matching a WHERE clause predicate without listing all partitions.
